topic_clusters.level1_desc: Understands that a logarithm asks 'what exponent?' Evaluates simple logarithms (log base 10 of 100). Cannot yet apply log properties.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: exponential equation, logarithmic equation, log properties, e, compound interest, half-life

topic_clusters.name: Polynomial Functions
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Applies the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra. Factors and solves higher-degree polynomials using synthetic division, rational root theorem, and complex zeros.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Graphs polynomial functions and identifies end behavior, zeros, and multiplicity. Divides polynomials using long division and synthetic division.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Graphs quadratic and cubic functions from a table. Identifies zeros from a graph. Performs polynomial long division with simple divisors.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Identifies the degree of a polynomial. Evaluates polynomials for given inputs. Recognizes that zeros are x-intercepts.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: end behavior, zeros, multiplicity, factor theorem, remainder theorem, synthetic division

topic_clusters.name: Probability & Combinatorics
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Applies conditional probability, Bayes' theorem concepts, and expected value to real-world decisions. Uses combinatorics to count complex outcomes.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Calculates permutations and combinations. Finds probabilities of compound events using addition and multiplication rules. Understands conditional probability and independence.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Distinguishes between permutations and combinations. Calculates probability of compound events with support. Uses tree diagrams and organized lists.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Finds probability of simple events. Uses the counting principle for two-stage experiments. Cannot yet distinguish permutations from combinations.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: factorial, independent events, conditional probability, mutually exclusive, complement, expected value

topic_clusters.name: Radical & Rational Exponents
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Solves equations involving radicals and rational exponents. Composes functions involving radicals. Connects radical and exponential forms fluently.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Rewrites expressions with rational exponents as radicals and vice versa. Simplifies radical expressions. Solves radical equations and checks for extraneous solutions.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Simplifies square roots. Converts between radical and exponential notation for simple cases. Solves basic radical equations.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Evaluates perfect square roots. Recognizes that a square root undoes squaring. Cannot yet simplify non-perfect-square radicals.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: radical, radicand, index, rational exponent, conjugate, extraneous solution, nth root

topic_clusters.name: Rational Expressions & Equations
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Solves complex rational equations and identifies extraneous solutions. Simplifies complex fractions. Models real-world problems with rational functions.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Simplifies rational expressions by factoring. Adds, subtracts, multiplies, and divides rational expressions. Solves rational equations.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Simplifies rational expressions with common monomial factors. Multiplies and divides rational expressions. Solves simple rational equations.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Identifies the domain restrictions of a rational expression. Simplifies fractions with numerical denominators. Cannot yet factor to simplify.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: extraneous solution, asymptote, common denominator, complex fraction

topic_clusters.name: Sequences & Series
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Derives and applies formulas for sums of arithmetic and geometric series, including infinite geometric series. Connects sequences to function families.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Identifies and writes explicit and recursive formulas for arithmetic and geometric sequences. Finds partial sums of arithmetic and geometric series.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Identifies arithmetic and geometric sequences. Finds the next term using the common difference or ratio. Writes explicit formulas with support.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Recognizes number patterns as sequences. Identifies the common difference in arithmetic sequences. Cannot yet write formulas.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: series, arithmetic, geometric, common difference, common ratio, explicit formula, recursive formula, partial sum, sigma notation

topic_clusters.name: Area, Surface Area & Volume
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Applies Cavalieri's principle. Solves optimization problems involving surface area and volume. Uses geometric modeling for real-world design problems.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Calculates surface area and volume of prisms, cylinders, pyramids, cones, and spheres. Identifies cross-sections of 3-D figures. Solves real-world problems.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Calculates volume and surface area of prisms and cylinders using formulas. Identifies basic cross-sections.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Finds volume of rectangular prisms and area of basic shapes. Cannot yet apply formulas for pyramids, cones, or spheres independently.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: cross-section, prism, cylinder, pyramid, cone, sphere, Cavalieri's principle, composite figure

topic_clusters.name: Circles
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Derives the equation of a circle from completing the square. Proves properties of inscribed and central angles. Solves complex problems involving arc length and sector area.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Identifies and uses properties of central angles, inscribed angles, arcs, chords, tangents, and secants. Finds arc length and sector area.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Identifies parts of a circle (radius, diameter, chord, arc, tangent). Finds arc measures from central angles. Uses circumference and area formulas.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Identifies radius, diameter, and circumference. Applies C = pi*d and A = pi*r^2 with support. Cannot yet work with arcs or inscribed angles.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: chord, tangent, secant, sector, arc length, inscribed polygon, circumscribed

topic_clusters.name: Coordinate Geometry
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Uses coordinates to prove geometric theorems algebraically (e.g., midpoint of hypotenuse, properties of parallelograms). Writes and graphs equations of circles.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Uses distance formula, midpoint formula, and slope to classify figures and verify properties. Partitions line segments in given ratios.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Applies the distance and midpoint formulas. Uses slope to determine parallel and perpendicular relationships.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Plots points and finds distances by counting on a grid. Recognizes that parallel lines have equal slopes. Cannot yet apply formulas fluently.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: slope, parallel, perpendicular, partition, coordinate proof, equation of a circle

topic_clusters.name: Geometric Proof & Reasoning
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Constructs multi-step formal proofs using deductive reasoning. Proves theorems about lines, angles, triangles, and parallelograms independently.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Writes two-column and paragraph proofs for congruent triangles and parallel line relationships. Uses postulates and theorems to justify geometric statements.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Completes two-column proofs with some steps provided. Identifies which theorem or postulate applies in straightforward cases.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Understands that proofs require logical steps. Can state given information but cannot yet construct a logical chain of reasoning.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: proof, theorem, postulate, axiom, conjecture, deductive reasoning, two-column proof, converse, contrapositive

topic_clusters.name: Similarity & Right Triangle Trigonometry
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Proves theorems involving similarity. Uses trigonometric ratios to solve multi-step real-world problems. Applies the Law of Sines and Cosines to non-right triangles.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Defines and uses sine, cosine, and tangent ratios to find missing sides and angles in right triangles. Understands and applies AA, SAS, and SSS similarity criteria.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Identifies similar triangles and sets up proportions to find missing sides. Uses trigonometric ratios with a calculator when given the setup.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Identifies corresponding parts of similar triangles. Knows SOH-CAH-TOA but cannot yet apply it independently to solve problems.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: similar, proportion, sine, cosine, tangent, opposite, adjacent, hypotenuse, angle of elevation, angle of depression

topic_clusters.name: Transformations & Congruence
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Describes sequences of transformations mapping one figure to another. Uses transformations to prove congruence and develop geometric theorems.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Performs translations, rotations (90/180/270), reflections, and dilations on the coordinate plane. Uses transformations to determine congruence and similarity.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Performs translations and reflections over axes. Identifies congruent figures. Applies single transformations with coordinates.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Recognizes translations, reflections, and rotations visually. Identifies congruent shapes by appearance. Cannot yet perform transformations with coordinates.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: reflection, dilation, congruent, rigid motion, pre-image, image, composition

topic_clusters.name: Counting & Cardinality
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Counts to 100 by 1s and 10s with accuracy. Compares and orders sets using precise language ("more than," "fewer than," "equal to"). Counts on from any given number and uses counting to solve simple joining/separating situations without prompting.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Counts to at least 20 objects with reliable one-to-one correspondence. Reads and writes numerals 0–20. Understands that the last number counted tells "how many" (cardinality). Compares two groups of up to 10 objects.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Counts to 10 with occasional errors. Recognizes some numerals and can match small quantities to numbers with support. Inconsistently applies one-to-one correspondence; may skip or double-count.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Rote counts to 5 with errors. Does not yet reliably connect counting words to objects. Needs physical manipulation and adult support to count small groups (1–5).
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: count, number, more, fewer, equal, how many

topic_clusters.name: Early Addition & Subtraction
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Fluently adds and subtracts within 10 using mental strategies. Represents and solves simple word problems (result unknown, change unknown) using drawings, equations, or objects. Decomposes numbers to 10 in multiple ways.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Solves addition and subtraction problems within 10 using objects, fingers, or drawings. Models joining and separating situations. Composes and decomposes numbers to 10 using concrete materials.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Adds and subtracts within 5 with concrete support. Understands "put together" and "take away" concepts but needs manipulatives. May not yet connect actions to number sentences.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Recognizes that groups can be combined or separated but cannot reliably find sums or differences, even within 5, without significant adult guidance.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: join, take away, put together, sum, difference

topic_clusters.name: Measurement & Comparison
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Compares and orders three or more objects by a measurable attribute (length, weight, capacity). Uses non-standard units to measure and describes results with numbers. Classifies objects by multiple attributes simultaneously.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Directly compares two objects using measurable attributes (longer/shorter, heavier/lighter). Describes differences using comparative language. Sorts objects into given categories and counts the number in each.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Compares two objects by length or size when placed side by side with support. Uses some comparative language but may confuse terms. Beginning to sort objects into categories.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Notices obvious size differences ("big" vs. "small") but cannot reliably compare objects by a specific attribute. Needs direct prompting and modeling to sort.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: long, short, heavy, light, tall, big, small

topic_clusters.name: Shapes & Spatial Sense
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Names and describes 2-D and 3-D shapes regardless of orientation or size. Composes new shapes from existing shapes. Describes relative positions using spatial language (above, below, beside, in front of).
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Identifies and names circles, triangles, rectangles, squares, and common 3-D shapes (cube, sphere, cone, cylinder). Sorts shapes by number of sides or corners. Describes basic attributes.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Identifies circles and squares reliably; inconsistent with triangles and rectangles. Recognizes some 3-D shapes but confuses names. Sorts with guidance.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Recognizes circles and squares in standard orientation only. Cannot consistently name other shapes. Needs physical models and significant support.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: square, cube, cone, sphere, cylinder, side, corner

topic_clusters.name: Sorting, Classifying & Data
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Collects, organizes, and represents data in simple graphs (pictographs, object graphs). Answers "how many" and "how many more/fewer" questions about the data. Proposes own categories for sorting.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Sorts and classifies objects into 2–3 given categories. Counts objects in each category. Answers simple questions about organized data ("Which group has more?").
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Sorts objects into two categories with support. Can count objects in one category but struggles to compare categories. Needs prompts to describe the data.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Recognizes that objects can be grouped but cannot independently sort by a given attribute. Needs direct modeling to participate in data collection activities.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: same, different, count

topic_clusters.name: Advanced Functions & Analysis
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Analyzes and graphs piecewise, absolute value, step, and rational functions. Performs operations on functions including composition and finds inverses of non-linear functions.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Identifies and graphs parent functions and their transformations. Composes functions and finds inverses algebraically. Analyzes end behavior and asymptotes.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Identifies parent function families (linear, quadratic, absolute value, square root). Applies vertical and horizontal shifts. Finds inverse of linear functions.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Evaluates functions for given inputs. Recognizes function notation. Identifies functions from graphs using the vertical line test.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: inverse, piecewise, step function, asymptote, end behavior, even/odd function

topic_clusters.name: Conic Sections
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Derives equations of conics from geometric definitions. Identifies conics from general second-degree equations. Solves systems involving conics.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Writes and graphs equations of parabolas, circles, ellipses, and hyperbolas in standard form. Identifies key features (center, foci, vertices, asymptotes).
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Graphs circles and parabolas in standard form. Identifies the type of conic from its equation. Finds center and radius of circles.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Recognizes the shapes of parabolas, circles, ellipses, and hyperbolas. Can graph a circle given center and radius on a grid.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: hyperbola, focus, vertex, directrix, center, major axis, minor axis, asymptote

topic_clusters.name: Limits & Introduction to Calculus
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Evaluates limits analytically using algebraic techniques (factoring, rationalizing, L'Hopital's rule concepts). Understands the limit definition of the derivative.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Evaluates limits graphically and numerically. Identifies continuous and discontinuous functions. Understands average and instantaneous rates of change.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Estimates limits from tables and graphs. Identifies points of discontinuity. Calculates average rate of change between two points.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Understands that a limit describes what a function approaches. Identifies where a function is undefined. Cannot yet evaluate limits.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: discontinuous, removable discontinuity, asymptote, average rate of change, instantaneous rate of change, derivative

topic_clusters.name: Trigonometric Functions
topic_clusters.level4_desc: Proves trigonometric identities. Models periodic phenomena with sinusoidal functions. Solves trigonometric equations on specified intervals.
topic_clusters.level3_desc: Evaluates trigonometric functions using the unit circle. Graphs sine, cosine, and tangent with amplitude, period, and phase shift. Converts between degrees and radians.
topic_clusters.level2_desc: Evaluates trig functions for standard angles. Graphs basic sine and cosine functions. Converts between degrees and radians with support.
topic_clusters.level1_desc: Knows the six trig ratios from right triangles. Evaluates trig functions for 0, 30, 45, 60, 90 degrees. Cannot yet use the unit circle.
topic_clusters.tier3_vocab: radian, sine, cosine, tangent, amplitude, period, phase shift, frequency, identity, sinusoidal

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Collaboration and relationship between distance and velocity
cpm_modules.notes:
eBook Scavenger Hunt Desmos
MN: Volumes of Standard Solids | Ideally, this lesson will be done as a synchronous meeting, as it is the first lesson of the year, and it is important to get to know each other and to set the norms and expectations for the year. In a virtual meeting, assign each team to a screen on this Shared Jamboard for 1.1.1. Refer to the Supplemental Resources eBook Teacher–Virtual Collaboration–Rough Draft Thinking tab for more information on collaboration using shared Google files. Join the Teaching CPM Remotely–Planning for Getting Started Remotely–Chapter 1 module at professionallearning.cpm.org for more support as you get started this year.

MN: Intuitive Notion of Continuity MN: Domain And Range Notation | Use the RI Calc 1.2.1 for this lesson (be sure to edit it to fit your own class's needs and upload your own teacher video introduction). This activity can be modified to fit other lessons as needed when you are teaching remotely. You can use the activity during a synchronous meeting, which will allow you to pause the class and present from your screen as appropriate for discussions.  It can also be use asynchronously, although we still encourage you to remind students to work with their classmates so they can discuss the mathematics and support one another's learning. If needed, skip 1-13, 1-16, 1-19.

MN: Horizontal Asymptotes | If done synchronously, use a jamboard (see 1.1.1 example) to have students share their thoughts as they compare the graphs in problem 1-29. Use Desmos to graph all three equations and discuss with the class. Prompt students to share their screens in breakout rooms as they use the Desmos eTool to explore the end behavior of graphs in problems 1-30 to 1-32. If working asyncronously, you can use the Desmos Remote Instruction Template to present each problem and have students share their conjectures regarding the structure of a rational equation and the characteristics of the graph. 1-35 is a good opportunity to check for understanding (if working asynchronously, you could collect student sketches using Calc 1-35 Responses. Encourage students to use the Desmos graphing calculators to check their work.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Holes, Vertical Asymptotes, and Approach Statements
cpm_modules.notes: MN: Approach Statements | Consider using a Virtual Think-ink-share during problem 1-44. You can manage responses using a virtual meeting chat, a Padlet, or the "Share With Class" option on a Desmos activity. Be sure to review the bulleted items on the suggested lesson activity of the ebook before moving forward. Use a Rough Draft Thinking Space for students to share their conjectures for problem 1-45. If needed, skip 1-49.

MN: Inverse Functions
INT3 CP 8: Writing Inverse Functions | The problems in this section are primarily review from previous courses. Consider allowing students to work through these problems in groups. If you are working in a synchronous meeting, circulate during breakout rooms. Make sure to close the lesson with the idea of how inverse functions can be verified using composition of functions. This can be done using a digital learning log or LMS discussion board if working asynchronously, or through a class discussion during a synchronous session. If needed, skip 1-65.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Attributes of Even and Odd Functions
cpm_modules.notes: MN: Even and Odd Functions | The problems in this section are primarily review from previous courses. Consider allowing students to work through these problems in groups. If you are working in a synchronous meeting, circulate during breakout rooms. Have students check each other's work for 78 using a Virtual Pairs Check. This can be done using a shared whiteboard space. If needed, skip 1-81

| This lesson is optional. Consider waiting to discuss flags in class until Chapter 8, when they are studied in depth as students learn about Volumes of Rotations. Students are supported in the concept of creating flags in Review & Preview problems throughout the chapters leading to Chapter 8.

| Each problem in this lesson leads to the Power Rule, and should not be skipped. Consider using Desmos to create a lesson using the Remote Instruction Template, using the tables and graphing features available in Desmos.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Slope Statements and Finite Differences of Non-Polynonials
cpm_modules.notes: MN: Common Trigonometric Identities | Have students share their slope statements on a shared Jamboard or othe type of document. For problem 112, consider assigning each team 2 functions and adding each team's work to a class shared Google Slide. Facilitate a Virtual Gallery Walk.

| Begin the lesson by having students watch this video which demonstrates a motion detector generating a distance graph. Consider doing a "Notice and Wonder" before students get started on 1-122. Assign each team 2-3 functions in problem 1-123, and then have them share their sketches and descriptions during a Virtual Gallery Walk. 1-124 is a good opportunity to check for understanding as students individually reflect.

| 1-135 is a problem that will be revisited again and will be used to develop the FTC. For that reason, try not to skip any problems in this lesson. It is important to take the time to connect this problem to the Freeway Fatalities problem in Lesson 1.1.1. Consider using a whole class Jamboard to facilitate a class discussion if you are working synchronously.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Average Velocity on a Position Graph
cpm_modules.notes: MN: Initial Position, Final Position, Displacement, and Total Distance | Use a Virtual Whiparound to facilitate discussion on average velocity in problem 1-148, and to discuss problem 1-151. This can be done on a shared whiteboard (such as Google Jamboard), or by having students unmute their mics one by one during an synchronous session. If needed, skip 1-150.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Average Velocity on a Velocity Graph
cpm_modules.notes: | 1-162 can either be done individually or in a group. This is a great opportunity to check for understanding. Be sure to provide feedback to students. Be prepared for students to mistake this graph for a distance graph. Emphasize that the graph repreesents velocity. If needed, skip 1-165.

MN: The Vocabulary of Motion | Each problem in this section is worthy of being done in a team. Anticipate that students will still struggle to realize that they are now exploring velocity graphs and not distance graphs.

| These problems are intended to help students consolidate the main concepts of this lesson as well as offer a preview of the Fundamental Theorem in Chapter 5, where they get analyzed thoroughly. They are not intended to produce mastery of these topics. Introduce the lesson with a Virtual Think-Pair-Share and then discuss as a class (or asynchronously in a discussion board). Problems 1-192 and 193 can be done using a shared team Jamboard (be sure students know they can draw straight lines if they hold the shift button while using the pen!) or other shared space. If students must work asynchronously, use the Desmos Remote Instruction Template to create a Desmos actiivity to collect the student responses so you can collect student work for a closure video.

MN: Trapezoid Rule
MN: Summation Notation | 2-1 can either be done as a whole class discussion or in groups. If you have students work in groups, come together and have stuents share their thinking for problems a-e. This can be done using a virtual whiparound. Circulate throughout breakout rooms as students work through problems 2-4 and 2-5, paying close attention to index, and the argument of their summation notation. Have pocket questions prepared as you circulate. If needed, skip 2-6

| Have students collaborate on 2-15 and 2-16. Consider using a Pairs Check to solidify student understanding of sigma notation, Students can communicate through a chat within your LMS or on a collaborative whiteboard space to do this. Have students begin problem 2-17 in their groups, and circulate throughout breakout rooms and provide support as needed. Direct students to the Desmos eTool for Estimating Area Under a Curve.  Bring students together for a class discussion to discuss the questions in 2-17c. This can be done using a virtual whip around. Consider using a Swapmeet or Pairs Check for students to provide feedback to one another for problem 2-18. This can be done using a shared space such as a Jamboard. Check for student understanding on 2-19c.If needed, skip 2-18c.

MN: Left Endpoint Rectangles | Problem 2-29 is a good place for students to asses their understanding of both expanded sums and using sigma notation. Have students work through this problem individually and make sure to provide feedback and then prompt students to correct their answers. Throughout this lesson, make sure to reinforce that Sigma Notation is only to be used for sums that include subintervals of equal width. Consider using a Pairs Check for Problem 2-31. Then, have each pair share their responses on a collaborative whiteboard as you discuss problem 2-32 as a class. If needed, skip 2-33 and 2-34.

MN: Limits | Problems 2-42 and 2-43 begin to develop the intuitive definition of a limit. Consider having students complete these problems on their own and discuss their solutions in a whole class discussion. If working syncronously with students, consider facilitating a whole class discussion to discuss Problem 2-44 and the Math Notes. If working asyncronously, consider asking students to summarize their understanding of the math notes and how they relate to problem 2-44. If needed skip 2-47.

| Lesson 2.2.2 develops the intuitive idean of Continuity, preparing students for the formal definition in 2.2.3. Many of the questions in this section ask students to explain and do not invole computation. For this reason, a Desmos Activity using the Remote Instruction Template would work well.   Consider pausing the activity to discuss problem 2-61 with students and point out that each disconty is caused by a different reason. 2-63 is a good place to formatively assesss students (do not expect mastery at this time)

MN: Continuity Conditions
MN: IVT | Begin by reading the Math Notes on The Formal Definition of Continuity with your students. Students should collaborate on problem 2-73 on a shared space. Provide them feedback. Facilitate a Hosted Gallery Walk and ask the Recorder/Reporter in each team to explain their graphs to the rest of the class. 2-77, 2-78 and 2-79 introduce The Intermediate Value Theorem. This concept will be woven throughout Chapter 2 and upcoming Chapters. If needed skip 2-80.

Precalculus CP 5: Rational Expressions (Supplemental Resource if needed) | Begin by having students complete problems 2-89 and 2-90 on a shared virtual space such as a jamboard. Encourage rough draft thinking. Students will need support in recalling various algebraic methods to simplifying rational expressions. If working syncronously with students, bring students back after giving them enough time to grapple with 2-90 and discuss 2-90 as a class. If working asyncronously with students, consider providing further guidance on 2-90 through video instruction, or directing them to Checkpoint 5 in Precalculus. Problem 2-94 can be used as a formative assessment at this time. If needed, skip 92 and 94

| Students can complete these problems independently using a Desmos calculator. Have students share a brief explanation, in their own words, of what the Squeeze Theorem means to check for understanding. You may choose to have students send those directly to you, or to use a single screen in Desmos so they can see some other student responses only after they have made their own response, and so you will have access to their responses to share with the class as appropriate.

| If you are in a syncronous setting, begin Problem 2-102 by using a Whip around or a Think-ink-share to allow students to brainstorm with oneanother. If you are in a syncronous setting you can have students brainstorm on a collaborative whiteboard. You can also use the Remote Instruction Desmos Template  and allow for students to see the responses of others. Problem 2-103 is a lab that involves a ramp and a rolling object. Consider completing the lab yourself before the lesson, filiming it, and providing data for the students. You can also film a video demonstrating how to set up the lab and ask students to try it at home, as this lab requires minimal materials. Consider using the The Remote Instruction Desmos Template to create a space for students to plot their data and complete the lab. Be sure to bring closure to this problem, and to bring student's attention to the variety of difference quotients that can be used to estimate the velocity at sudden impact. Once students have calculated a velocity, press them by asking how they could refine their estimation. The idea of a difference quotient will be used to develop the idea of a Derivative later on in Chapter 3.

| Students should work collaboratively on 2-112. Use a collaborative space such as jamboard in breakout rooms, and encourage rough draft thinking. Anticipate that students will need support in identifying the Position function. Consider bringing students together and modeling this during a whole class discussion. You may also choosse to provide students with this equation. If you do choose to give this equation to student, make sure that they are able to justify why it is quadratic. Encourage students to use Desmos to plot the position data and compare it with the position function.

MN: Trig Limits
MN: Local Linearity | Desmos is the ideal tool for this lesson, as students will be examining functions when zoomed in very close. Make sure to bring students together to discuss the local linearity of y= |x| at different points such as (0,0), (2, 2) and (-4, 4). Students have not been exposed to differentiability yet, so expect a lively discussion about this, introducing the word cusp. Consider using a Teammates Consult to promote discussion for problem 2-124. If needed, you may skip this lesson.
| Direct students to the  Estimating Area Under a Curve eTool  as they complete problem 2-132. If need, you may skip this lesson.

| Provide guidance for students in problem 3-1 to support them in sketching their tangent lines correctly and as accurately as possible (use the 3-1 eTool to demonstrate or save time on this problem). Students can work collabortively through 3-2 to 3-6. 3-7 can be skipped if needed. 3-8 is a good place to check for individual understanding. This lesson introdcues the idea of a "slope function", which will be used to later formalize the idea of a derivative. Consider doing every problem in this lesson.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Secants to Tangents, AROC to IROC
cpm_modules.notes:
MN: Power Rule
MN: AROC and IROC | Problem 3-22 can be a discussion. Prepare sketches of the secant lines ahead of time. 3-23 should be done as a class. The connection between AROC and IROC is critical in the development of the Derivative in the upcoming lesson. For that reason, consider doing every problem in this lesson.

MN: Derivative | Problem 3-36 can be condensed into a discussion. If needed, skip 3-36, but be mindful that using left, right and symmetric difference quotients to find the derivative will all be revisited in Review and Preview problems. Challenge teams to complete 3-38, but be sure to discuss 3-38 as a class. 3-39 can be mentioned when going over the Math Notes. Problem 3-40 is a good example of how to use the Limit Definition of a Derivative and should be done as a class. 3-41 can be skipped if needed, as long as students do 3-55 in Lesson 3.2.2.

| Have students complete problem 2-52 using a Pairs check or a Think-ink-pair-share. This can be done by creating a shared Google Document where students can let each other know what time they work on math. They can use that and a chat within your LMS to discuss problems on a shared whiteboard space such as a Google Jamboard, each using a different color. Bring students together to discuss problem 3-52 as a class. Skip 3-57.

| You can complete 3-68 using a virtual Whiparound. Problem 3-69 is an algebraic proof of the derivative of sin(x) and cos(x) while 3-70 demonstrates their derivatives graphically. Consider skipping 3-69 and spending more time on 3-70. If needed, skip 3-71 and 3-72

| Consider having students collaborate on a shared drawing space such as a jamboard for the majority of this lesson. Incorporate STTS such as Think-Ink-Pair-Share or Peer Edit to provide students feedback and to encourage discussion.

MN: Curve Analysis | At this point, students are still building understanding about the relationships between graphs and their derivatives. Allow students to collaborate as much as possible so that they can recieve constant feedback from one another. If needed, skip 3-100, although this problem can easily be done through a quick discussion.

| Expect that students will need guidance on problem 3-111. Consider providing students with a sketch of the first graph for problem 3-111 (or use a shared Google Jamboard 3-111 with the Resource Page as the background for the board) and focusing on supporting students in sketching the tangent lines and using them to sketch the subsequent graphs. It is advantagous for each graph to remain vertically aligned in this lesson. If working syncronously with students, consider using a whole class discussion with students to work through this problem. If working asyncronously with students, it may be beneficial to provide a sketch of the first two graphs, and ask students to make connections between them, before having students finish the third graph individually.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Ways to Describe f′ and f′′
cpm_modules.notes: MN: Derivative Notation | This lesson will take two days. Problem 3-124 can be done using a Jamboard. This can be done both synronously or asyncronously. If you are in a syncronous setting, you can model the walk in problem 3-125. If not, consider filming yourself, or refer to this video that demonstrates a walk using a motion detector.

MN: Antiderivatives | If working in a syncronous setting, consider using breakout rooms to facilitate a Jigsaw for problem 3-145. Make sure to close the lesson by reviewing the different cases of non-differentiability. 3-146 can be done through a class discussion.

MN: Differentiability | If needed, skip 3-156 as long as you feel that students understood this concept in the previous day's lesson. Consider facilitating problem 3-157 through a whole class discussion, and demonstrating how to make use of the "zoom" feature on the calculator. Problem 3-160 can be done using a virtual whiparound.
| Consider facilitating a Hosted Gallery Walk for problem 3-172, and asking the recorder/reporter to share out about their team's sketch. If needed, skip 3-173.

| This problem serves to unify the ideas built in this chapter and provides students the opportunity to apply what they have learned in this chapter about tangent lines to solve a problem. While this problem is a very worthwhile task, given the circumstances, you may consider skipping it, and offering it as an additional problem-solving opportunity.
MN: Definite Integrals | Have students share their conclusions for 4-1 in a virtual Whiparound. Bring students together to discuss probelm 4-3 as well as the Math Notes on Definite Integrals.

| If you are teaching an AP Calculus course, students should be proficient at using their calculators to evaluate definite integrals. As you circulate, check to see that students know how to use the integration feature on their calculator to calculate definite integrals. If you are virtual, students can search Youtube for calculator demonstrations, or even make their own!
MN: Definite Integrals with Variable Bounds | Consider bringing groups together for a whole class discussion for problem 4-53 and ask each team to share their findings.
MN: Indefintite Integrals
MN: FTOC | If needed, skip 4-82
| If needed, skip 4-96

| Section 4.3 features a unifying problem for the chapter where students will be reviewing the concepts learned in this chapter. Students should complete these problems. To save time during this section, reduce the amount of time you spend discussing the mock trial, and focus on the mathematics content in the problems. Have students read problems 4-106 and 4-107 individually and then bring the class together to discuss the details of the trial by facilitating a class Whip-Around. Desmos is a good tool to use on problem 4-108 and throughout this section to facilitate collaboration. Consider creating a Desmos Class activity or providing students with Desmos graphs with the equations pre-loaded like this example here.

| Have students work on 4-117 and 4-118 in teams. You may incorporate the mock trial if time, otherwise, make sure to provide closure to this lesson by having students point out the discontunity in the graph at 0.8 hours. Click here for a Desmos graph with pre-loaded functions for this lesson.

| Have students work on 4-127 in teams. If needed, you may skip the closing arguments, however, you should make sure to close out this section through whole class discussion, or by having students discuss the problems in teams and sharing their findings with the class.

| Bring students together for problems 4-130, and 4-131 to discuss negative area. All of these problems are essential and students should work through all of them. To save time, consider choosing 1 or 2 integrals and allowing students to evaluate them using the integral feauture on their calculator, instead of calculating them by hand. 4-133 is a good opportunity for formative assessment.
| If needed, skip 4-145

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Multiple Methods for Calculating Area Between Curves
cpm_modules.notes: MN: Area Between Curves | Each problem in this section expands on 4-155. It may be beneficial to bring students together to discuss this problem before moving forward. It is not necessary to have every team present their solution for problem 4-157. To save time, purposefully circulate among groups and choose 2-3 groups to share their solutions. If needed, skip 4-158 and 4-159.
MN: Newton's Method | To ensure students have sufficient time to grasp the fundamentals of this course, this is a good year to skip this section.

| Students should use Desmos throughout this lesson to graph h(t), v(t) and a(t) on the same set of axis and compare them. 5-4 summarizes the relationship between h(t), v(t), and a(t). It can be used as a closure and is best teacher led as a whole class.

| Problem 5-13 involves cutting the 5.1.2 Resource page to model 4 rectangular boxes. Provide students with access to this resource page ahead of time, so that they can print it and have it available for the lesson. Alternatively, consider providing students with a recording of you folding each box. Before showing the recording, facilitate a class whip-around using a Jamboard or Padlet using guiding questions provided in the Teacher Notes. You may assign groups just 1 of the 4 boxes to save time.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Using the First and Second Derivatives
cpm_modules.notes: MN: Extreme Values | Use a common virtual space such as a Jamboard for students to do a Think-Ink-Pair Share for problem 5-21. Have students compare their responses in their teams, and once each team has agreed on their response, have them share out as a class. Repeat this process for problem 5-22. Problem 5-24 introduces a lot of important vocabulary from this chapter. You may consider bringing students together as a whole class to discuss these terms, or, complete 5-24 as a whole class.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Applying the First and Second Derivative Tests
cpm_modules.notes: MN: Critical Points and the Terivative Tests for Extrema | Consider having students collaborate in pairs or teams on a shared space for problems 33 and 34. Problem 5-33 introduces students to the conditions for the First Derivative Test. Make sure to check for understanding and provide students opportunities to share and summarize their findings in this problem. Problem 34 can also be done as a whole class discussion where students share their thoughts in a public chat space.  Moniter break out rooms during this time to check for understanding and misconceptions. Encourage students to use Desmos, or a rough draft thinking space such as a Jamboard to illustrate their thinking.
MN: The Product Rule | Consider teaching students how to verify their answers graphically using Desmos or a graphing calculator (See the Suggested Lesson Activity in Teacher Notes of the eBook)

cpm_modules.core_concepts: The Chain Rule and Application: Part One
cpm_modules.notes: MN: The Chain Rule | Lesson 5.2.2 is long. Consider splitting it into two days if needed. 5-59 can be done as a whole class discussion or in teams or pairs using a think-ink-share. If needed, skip 5-60. Students are developing The Chain Rule in Problems 5-61 to 5-64. Make sure to closely monitor Break out Rooms during these problems to check for understanding. Bring students together to discuss their findings in 5-61 to 5-63, and to discuss their conclusions in problem 5-64.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: The Chain Rule and Application: Part Two
cpm_modules.notes: | Problem 5-76 can be done as a Warm up to check for individual understanding. Problem 5-77 combines multiple derivative rules. Consider having students work in teams to complete this problem. Problem 5-78 asks students to find dL/dt. Derivative notation will be important in Chapter 7 when students are introduced to Related Rates.

MN: The Quotient Rule | Problems 5-87 and 5-88 can be done individually and are considered review material. In Problem 5-89, students will derive the Quotient Rule for the first time. Consider having students complete problem 5-89 in pairs or in teams using a collaborative whiteboard space. Be sure to closly monitor Breakout rooms to check for understanding. Bring students together as a class to share their findings and to ensure understanding before moving on to 5-90. If needed, skip 5-91.

| Discuss 5-100 with students as a class, to ensure understanding of each expression before moving to 5-101. If working syncronously with students, consider differentiating y=tan(x) with students as a class in problem 5-101. Have students complete the remainder of Problem 5-101 in teams. You may assign each team to one function only to save time. Problem 5-102 can be done both in teams or individually. If needed, choose only some of the functions to have students practice differentiation. This is a good problem to check for understanding.

| If done synchronously, both problems in this lesson will require some teacher guidance, as well as routine checking for understanding. Consider having students begin problem 5-110 in breakout rooms using a collaborative white board space to share their initial thoughts and rough draft thinking. After some time, pull students together to discuss each problem. Repeat this process for Problem 5-111

| Like Lesson 5.3.1, each problem in this lesson will require some teacher guidance and routine circulation for checking for understanding. Consider giving students time to begin the problem in break out rooms, and make sure to provide feedback in a whole-group setting. If needed, skip 5-121.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: The Chain Rule Extension of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
cpm_modules.notes: | Make sure to discuss conclusions for 5-138 and 5-1139 as a class before moving on to 5-140. If needed, skip 5-140c, f

| Encourage students to use the 'Zoom' feature in Desmos to analyze the limits in problems 5-148 to 5-151. 5-150 develops the concept of L'Hospital's Rule, which will be applied in the following lesson. Consider guiding students through this problem if you are asyncronous. If needed, skip 5-153.

MN: Using l'Hospital's Rule | Students can complete Problem 5-162 on a shared whiteboard space using a Pairs Check. Bring students together as a class to discuss their findings in problem 5-163 where L'Hospital's Rule must be applied twice. 5-165 is a good place to check for understanding.  If needed, skip 5-166 and 5-167.
MN: All About "e" and e^x" | If needed, skip 6-6

| Consider having students first reflect individually on Problem 6-17, and having them share their responses on a shared space such as a google document. Problems 6-21 and 6-22 are a good place to check for understanding. If needed, have students only complete part of 6-21 and 6-22.

Derivatives of Exponential Functions | If students are completing Problem 6-32 in breakout rooms, make sure to circulate and check for understanding. Encourage students to check their solutions using their calculators, or by graphing, and consider any additional ways you will provide them feedback.

| Have students work through 6-41 in breakout rooms and plan to have students share their responses with the rest of the class. 6-45 can be completed in groups or independently. If needed, you may skip part of 6-45.
| Skip 6-58 if needed.
| Students can work through Problem 6-67 before meeting syncronously but plan to discuss this problem with students. If needed, skip problem 6-69
| Skiip 6-79 and 6-84 if needed. This can be a challenging lesson for students. Be sure to bring students together to check for understanding throughout this lesson.
| 6-93 is a good place to check for student understanding. Be sure to provide students feedback on their work for these problems.
| Skip 6-105 and 6-106 if needed. If students are working on 6-101 in breakoutrooms, consider using a shared virtual whiteboard space for students to collaborate and sketch tangent lines on.

| For Problem 6-113, consider how students will submit their responses regarding the number of graduation tickets theyn may need. You may consider having students submit their responses prior to the lesson and creating a bar graph to present during a class discussion. You may also have students use the post-it feature on a Jamboard or Padlet.

MN: Average (Mean) Values
MN: The Mean Value Theorem | Skip 6-131 and 6-132 if needed. Be sure to review the Math Notes with Students.

Rolle's Theorem | This is a long lesson. You may need to plan for two class periods for this lesson. If you assign problems 6-147 to 6-150 to be done asychronously, be sure to provide students with feedback as these problems cover critical ideas and theorems from this course.
Improper Integrals | This lesson is a part of the Calculus BC curriculum and should be skipped.
| Consider having students complete 7-1 before the lesson, and having them share their findings in a whole-class discussion.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Related Rates Application: The Pythagorean Theorem
cpm_modules.notes: | Students will need guidance on Related Rates Problems throughout this chapter. Consider giving students time to begin the problems in their breakout rooms, and then bringing them back to discuss the problems as a class.

| Allow students begin problem 7-24 in their breakout rooms. Provide feedback. Consider the prior knowledge students will need to be successful in this lesson (i.e. similar triangles, volume of a cone)

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Related Rates Applications: Choosing the Best Formula
cpm_modules.notes: Solving Related Rates Problems | Now that students have had more exposure to related rates problems, give them time to complete 7-34 and 7-36 in teams. Prepare a closure activity for students to recieve feedback and share their thinking as a whole-class.If needed, skip 7-35.
| Give students time to work on 7-53 in teams and bring them together to discuss their findings. Do not teach The Chain Rule today.

Substitution Method for Integration | 7-64 is a part of the Related Rates Thread that continues on throughout this chapter. Allow students to do this in groups, but make sure that you are providing guidance and feedback when necessary, as students are still developing mastery.
| If needed, skip 7-76.
| If needed, skip 7-87 and 7-88
Solving Differential Equations | 7-96 and 7-97 can be done in teams or individually, but both need to be discussed as a class. Review the Math Notes with students.
| This lesson involes a lab that provides another context for students to think about changing rates. If needed, you may skip this lesson or problem 7-110.

| Problems 7-125, 7-126 and 7-128 can be done in teams, but should also be addressed as a class. If teams were struggling with solving differential equations during Lesson 7.3.2, consider providing more guidance and/or completing one or more of these problems as a class. 7-129 can be used as a formateive assessment on solving differential equations

Once students have had sufficient time to practice drawing slope fields, be sure to shift the emphasis on analyzing slope fields, interpretting their characteristics, and connecting them back to the original function. This lesson really needs to be done in person in order to place the data on the graphs. An alternative is to have students share the information with you and for you to create the graph. After putting all the data on the graph you can take a picture or screen shot and share it with your students.

Alternate activities that can be done for remote learning to develop a sense of community:
1) Name Tent
2) Personal Classroom Space 

If you choose not to do this lesson, be sure to embed team expectations into your first several meetings.

The eTool allows for students to be able to do this in a remote, virtual environment. If not meeting in person, this is a great option.

Problem 1-7 has its own eTool depending on the figure -- A through E -- the teams are assigned.

Problem 1-16 has teams create a poster. If not meeting in person, skip problems 1-16 and 1-17 and have students work on problem 1-18.

Problem 1-15 and 1-18 each have their own eTool, which will help students.

This is one of the most memorable problems in this course. If you have meet together in person, this is a good lesson, as it also sets up skills for later in the course.

If you meet remotely, skip problem 1-24 as it asks teams to make decisions. The data on the Resource Page needs to be shared.

Problem 1-26 mentions the book If You Hopped Like a Frog. Consider a read-aloud or show a video reading of the book to introduce this problem.

This lesson is a hands-on lesson where students draw out beans (peanuts) from a bag. Unless they are in class together, it will be difficult to do this. In addition, since this involves a lot of handling of materials, it is best to avoid this lesson in this environment.

This lesson is a hands-on lesson where students have piles of pennies. Unless they are in class together, it will be difficult to do this. In addition, since this involves a lot of handling of materials, it is best to avoid this lesson in this environment.

If you feel the need to do this lesson, consider this Desmos activity: RI CC1 Lesson 1.2.1 (Sample).
If you skipped Lesson 1.2.1, then problem 1-52 should also be skipped here.
Problems 1-73 through 1-75 give students time to explore the table and allow for them to recognize patterns. However, in a reduced year capacity, it is best to skip these.

This lesson has students place data on a bar graph and Venn diagram. If not in person, consider using this Desmos activity: How Many Pets?.

Note, however, that the activity will graph the class data. This works only if it is synchronously done -- meaning the class must be on Desmos at the same time.

This lesson needs class data. If not meeting together, this is difficult. Conisder creating your own set of data and have students work problems 2-12 and 2-13 with that. 

The discussion of stem-and-leaf plots and historgrams should be facilitated by the teacher.

Also, this Desmos Activity: Creating Histograms can be used as an alternate way to teach this lesson.

This lesson has students measure their desk. In a remote environment, they could measure their work space that they use in their home. Allow them to be creative with this -- sticky notes, note cards, pencils, paper clips, etc.

Part (e) of problem 2-22 can be an extension or the first part of your review/preview.
Problem 2-32 is best when teacher facilitated, since the figure does not include right angles and needs to be estimated. Consider using 2-33 instead (as noted in the reduced year problems column).
For problems 2-39 and 2-40 in a remote environment, consider using this web app. If you choose this option have students screen shot their work so that you can check in with them.

For problem 2-51, consider using this app for a random draw of 5 cards.

The emphasis here on the generic rectangle will be useful throughout this and future courses. This will lead into the next lesson.

Consider beginning with a Math Talk to begin this lesson and the next two lessons. Specifically, choose a number talk where mental math can be done using the Distributive Property.

If you feel students need more pracitce, here is a Desmos activity designed to allow multiple practice problems: Multi-Digit Multiplication and the Generic Rectangle
Problem 2-70 is best facilitated by the teacher as it introduces the idea of the Greatest Common Factor.

If students struggle on problems 2-80 and 2-81, refer to the previous lesson. Take time to check in with students on problem 2-82. This formalizes what they have been doing with the Generic Rectangle.

The Giant One is a concept that will be very important through the middle school courses. Take time to check in that students understand this. Problems 3-5 and 3-6 give practice with this.

Problems 3-7 and 3-8 are the reverse to simplify fractions. In a reduced year capacity, this may not be as vital in which to spend valuable time when together.
For problem 3-27 you will need to give them a number for part (b). Helping students use both the Giant One and the Percent Ruler will benefit them throughout middle school.

Emphasize connections between the parts of the diagram and the numbers in the equation. For example, when Riley prepares 1/3 of 1/2, note the 2 parts of the whole mural, the 3 parts of that section, and that the finished piece is 1 of 6 equal parts in the whole mural.
Skip Sonya's diagram. Discuss benefits of different diagrams. Discuss connections between diagram and algorithm. If students make solid connections, consider skipping some of the practice problems.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Connection Diagrams to Algorithm for Fraction Multiplication
cpm_modules.notes: Emphasize connections between the parts of the diagram and the numbers in the equation, using students' voices or work. Use 5-19 to articulate how the total area of the diagram is the product of the numerators, and the overlapping area is the product of the numerators.

Section 5.2 will develop decimal multiplication, so focus on mixed numbers here. Estimation is important to help students see that multiplying with mixed numbers can yield products greater than they expect. Emphasize the regions of the generic rectangles to show how that product is greater.

Have students explicitly emphasize connections between multiplying fractions and multiplying decimals. Use the generic rectangle work in 5-40 and 5-41 to emphasize the value of the denominator and the place value of the decimal. Use color coding to connect the generic rectangle to the partial products equation and the algorithm in 5-42 and 5-43. Have students reflect on the patterns in the place value as they work with the algorithm. This is the only lesson with decimal algorithm work, so revisit it frequently in Review and Preview.
5-54 is a fun team challenge that reviews many fraction multiplication skills and understandings.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Decompose shapes into rectangles to find area
cpm_modules.notes: Consider replacing this lesson with the Desmos activity in the Teacher Notes. No matter what, focus conversations around geometric vocabulary and the idea that complex shapes can be rearranged into shapes you know how to find the area of, and the next few lessons will help students discover area formulas for polygons by connecting them to rectangles. Make sure students use base and height going forward.

Use 5-76 etools for student work. Heavily emphasize how each shape can become a rectangle or parallelogram. Emphasize that parallelograms need a small shift to become rectangles, and therefore the area formulas are the same.
Closely monitor student discussion and work to focus on the idea and visualization of triangles as half the area of parallelograms.

Use the etools for 5-96 and spend time bringing that question to closure in the Learning Log. Students do not need to include an area formula, but should have strong visualizations of how a trapezoid might become a parallelogram. The Math Notes on Trapezoid Area are in 6.1.1

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Quotients can be expressed as addition expressions, fractions greater than 1, and mixed numbers
cpm_modules.notes: A solid discussion about student work makes this lesson go well. Having students note each team's licorice, people, and quotient as both fraction and mixed number helps to bring out the connection that all division problems can be expressed as fractions. Expecting listeners to ask how or why questions about presented work also helps structure the discussion

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Consider meanings of numbers in a division problem as what they represent changes
cpm_modules.notes: If your students are struggling with long division, it might be worth taking the time to connect 7 divided by 5 in the assorted diagrams and to the algorithm. If they are not struggling, you could skip this lesson.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Dividing by unit fractions is equivalent to multiplying by the denominator
cpm_modules.notes: The concrete model of the pies will be important to students as they move through fraction division. Make sure they label numbers as they work: "3 pies divided into 1/8 size slices equals 24 total slices". Lean into students' insistance it is 3x8 to connect that dividing by unit fractions feels like multiplying. This will be further developed in Ch. 7
6-44 is a great assessment of students' understanding of dividing by unit fractions. 6-45 pushes students to consider how many 3/4s are in 8. You could wait to do this in Ch 7.
Assign 6-62 ahead of time, then support teams through 6-63 to 6-65
6.2.2 and 6.2.3 can be combined using the slide deck in the Sharing tab. If students are using physical tiles, do the recommended problems.

A good discussion of work from 6.2.2 will help students present the idea of combining like terms to each other. Allow teams time to work on the Shape Challenges and share work using some form of a Gallery Walk (one is formatted in the slides in the Sharing tab.
If students are already good at combining like terms, you could emphasize vocabulary.
The Desmos etool linked in the ebook is excellent for exploring the effect of different values of variables.

Find ways to elicit and share student work that highlights various comparison strategies, such as Google Slides gallery walk or selecting teams to give brief presentations to the whole class. Selecting students who started to use unit rates will help prepare students for upcoming lessons.

Make sure students have Desmos accounts so they can save their work and turn it in to you. 7-16 makes a good warm up or closure to examine how speed is shown on a graph. Consider using just this lesson or 7.1.3 to address graphs and unit rate.
Students can save work in Desmos. Consider skipping this if 7.1.2 fulfilled the Learning Intent, or doing just this lesson if students like the context.
Students examine how many of the divisor fit into the dividend and build their number sense for fraction division.
The strategies used in this section informally lead students toward the fraction division algorithm. You could skip this section, or use the problems to build number sense.

This lesson needs teacher support, but students could work in shared sides on practice problems. 7-62 could be a Silent Debate using Chat, and a whole class Learning Log could also be drafted using Chat.

7-69 can be done as a Carousel since the order of the problems do not matter. Consider posting the problems on a Jamboard and having students rotate through the slides.Or, you could Launch with students writing a division sentence for each problem and then working with partners or teams to apply strategies to solve each. You could use private chat for a Red Light Green Light.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Mean and Median measure the center of data, Range and Outliers describe spread.
cpm_modules.notes: Emphasize the concept of a distribution and the usefulness of summary measures over calculating them by hand. Teaching students to use spreadsheets or tools like stats.cpm.org to generate graphs and descriptive statitics will allow you to spend more time on analysis and interpretation. 6th Graders are generally studying the question "What is typical?" i.e. What is the typical size of family in our class? What is the typical temperature on Nov 1?

Problem 1-10 asks the essential question, What makes a fair game? Then, teams create their own rules to do this. This is best facilitated by the teacher if done virtually.

This Desmos activity: RI CC2 Lesson 1.1.2 can be used with your remote instruction, either synchronously or asynchronously.

This lesson is an introduction to solving equations by working backwards (reversing your thinking).  To add depth, ask students to write their mental computations as an equation with variables.  Consider a Splat! Talk as a way to introduce this lesson.

Problem 1-23 can be done before meeting together. In problem 1-25 students measure their own pennies. If done remotely consider measuring the stack of pennies for them while meeting synchronously or making a video of you measuring the stack of pennines asynchronously. In either case the goal is to give them the number of pennies that measure 1 cm in height (7 pennies).

Problem 1-29 gives an alternative way to solve. Problems 1-30 and 1-31 assess the accuracy of the predictions from the beginning of class. If time is short or you are meeting virtually, these can be skipped.
Problem 1-40 should be completed before meeting. 1-43 and 1-44 might need a check in as students are introduced to vocabulary.

The eTools with the spinner or the bag are a good way to allow asynchronous work to be done while visualizing the math.

One virtual alternative is to use the Desmos lesson Chance Experiments. Thought most of this lesson would then be done indpendently, the teacher dashboard allows you to check on student work.

Problems 1-63 through 1-66 should be teacher-facilitated. It is best done in the classroom, but a synchronous viritual environment is possible. If limited to asynchronous, consider only problems 1-67 and 1-68.

This doc showing a standard deck of cards might be useful for them.
Use this eTool for students to draw from the bag. This can eb done in a remote environment or in the classroom.

This lesson is an optional lesson to revisit conversion of fractions to percents, which is a 6th grade standard. It is used within the context of probability.  However, in a reduced year capacity, it can be skipped and used as remediation if necessary.
This video: Fraction to Percent in problem 1-95 can be helpful to visualize the process.

This lesson revisits fraction addition with unlike denominators, which is a 5th grade standard.  It is used within the context of adding outcomes in probability.  It can be skipped and used as remediation if necessary.

The student eTool is helpful to visualize the deck of cards. Note: many students do not know what a standard deck of cards contains. This doc showing a standard deck of cards might be useful for them.
The Probability eTool can help simulate drawing from the bag.  This eTool works well either virtually or in the classroom.

Problem 2-1 can be assigned before meeting together. The video for problem 2-2 can be used remotely, since this is a difficult problem for students to do on their own. Even then, it can get confusing. 

Consider that this problem 2-2 should be facilitated by the teacher and explained for students.

Problem 2-18 can be assigned before mneeting together. Part (d) asks them to work with their team to explain. If doing this virtually, this could be doen individually as well.

The resource pages are not necessary in a virtual envirnoment, as the decimals are included in the problem. Adjust the instructions given to students if this is the case.

Problem 2-31 has an eTool that is wonderful for students to use. If together, have students work in pairs to do this. If virtual, students can work independently and then share later. Remind students to write down Cecil's movements.

Problem 2-34 centers on the discussion. This is best if it is teacher-facilitated. If this is not possible, consider how you might have this discussion with students virtually.

This Desmos activity can be used to help students understant adding positive and negative values. However, since the number line is vertical, it may not have the same effect as the activity with Cecil.

This lesson can be benefit from using the eTool from Lesson 2.2.1 with Cecil. See notes on Lesson 2.2.2.

Problem 2-45 revisits absolute value, but does so in a different context.  This discussion can be confusing for students and thus is best facilitated by the teacher.

Problem 2-57 is better done in person. However, this is an excellent game for students to practice their skill in adding integers, but is difficult. If in person, this is a good way to engage students and help them work on an important skill.

Consider using the eTool for problem 2-58. If in person, this is a better option to avoid touching things. If done remotely, they will not have access to the pyhsical integer tiles.
Problem 2-67 can be done before meeting together. Problem 2-74 is best facilitated by the teacher with the discussion.

Problem 2-86 introduces the Unit Rectangle, which is new to students. In previous courses they have used the Generic Rectangle. This discussion is best facilitated by the teacher.

Problems 2-87 and 2-88 allow students to work with this concept. Problem 2-89 is more practice with this concept.

The key concept in this lesson is to multiply mixed numbers. Thus, the focus should be on problems 2-97 and 2-98.

The Unit Rectangle helps student to visually conceptualize this skill. In doing so, it can help prevent many common errors that students make.

Problems 2-107 through 2-109 need graphs. Consider using Desmos to do this. Problem 2-110 allows students to see that they can make predictions from graphs.
