This lesson is a follow-up on Lesson 2.3.1 on graphing and choosing an appropriate scale for the graph. If students seem to understand the concept from the previous lesson, then this one is not a necessity.

It can be used for remediation or for skill development at any point later in the course.

Problems 3-1 and 3-2 can be assigned before meeting together. Problem 3-3 is best when facilitated by the teacher. Problems 3-4 and 3-5 allow students to practice the skill.

Problem 3-5 is an extension that allows for multiple pathways and multiple answers. If you use this problem in a remote environment, find a way for students to share their answers.

Problem 3-12 is a difficult problem for students to write a diagram -- specifically a term with an exponent of 3. This problem is best facilitated by the teacher.

Problem 3-16 is an extension that allows for multiple pathways and multiple answers. Remotely, you can use this Desmos activity as an alternative.
Subtraction of negative integers is a key concept. See note on Lesson 3.2.1/3.2.2.
The key skill here is to re-write subtraction as addition of the opposite. This lesson can easily be comined with Lesson 3.2.1 and subtraction of integers. See note on Lesson 3.2.1/3.2.2.

Problem 3-54 follows from problem 3-53. However, it is difficult for students to conceptualize and understand a negative number of groups of negative numbers, such as -7(-11). This would be best with teacher facilitated discussion.

Problems 3-54 and 3-55 ask for teams to prepare a presentation or a poster. If done remotely, consider how you want students to deomstrate their understanding.

If you do this lesson remotely and choose to require problem 3-64, the eTool is a good way for them to do this problem.

Problem 3-67 will give you a good idea of the students' number sense with regards to multiplication of decimals.

This lesson brings together the work done in chapter 2 and 3 in regards to integers. The Tug-O-War game is a great way to engage students and have them practice skills. Students could use this remotely and write down observations, but they will not be playing with other students at that point.

However, in a reduced year capacity, it can be skipped and used as remediation if necessary.
Problem 3-87 should be completed before meeting together.  See note on Lesson 3.3.1.
Problem 3-98 should be complete before meeting. Problems 3-100 and 3-102 might need to be checked to assure that students are understanding the concept.
Problem 3-113 should be facilitated by the teacher, focusing on the Commutative and Associative Properties. This should be the focus of this lesson.

There is dot paper as a Resource Page that students will need to have printed. If this lesson is virtual, think about how to do this. There are a couple of online apps, but they are isometric, which will not really work here. Consider if students have the capability to print these out or if there is a way to draw online.

Problem 4-4 can be done without drawing anything.
If done virtually, consider using this Desmos activity. One emphasis should be on the scale factor as a multiplier.

If time is short, do not spend time on Gustavo's bank account (problems 4-21, 4-22, 4-24). The Desmos eTool is a great tool to use here.

Focus on the definition of proportional relationship in problem 4-26.

The Desmos eTool is great to use here. In problem 4-37 the emphasis should be that proportional relationships require a constant multiplier.

Consider using this Desmos activity as a formative assessment tool at the end of the lesson.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Determining Proportionality and Calculating Unit Rates
cpm_modules.notes: There are a few Desmos activities that could be done with this lesson: Click Battle and The Yogurt Shop. They enhance the lesson but should not replace the lesson.

Consider using Desmos for the graphs and have students share screen shots with you.

Problem 4-58 asks for teams to make a poster to cement the connections of the proportions web. Consider asking students to make these and share them virtually.

If you choose to do this lesson as written, it needs to be done synchronously (in person or video) as it introduces Algebra Tiles.  If your choice is to do this lesson asynchronously, make sure that the students have the correct name/label of each Algebra tile.

The eTool with Algebra tiles is very helpful in a remote, virtual environment.

Problem 4-87 needs to be facilitated by the teacher, as the right side is equal to (x – 2). This is not intuitive to every student and can be difficult for some students.

4-90 might need discussion as many students have not yet mastered the Distributive Property.
A quick check in with students to make sure they understand the concept of zero pairs should be sufficient.

Assigning 5-1 to 5-4 to teams or individuals will help you facilitate 5-5 and 5-6. Percent relationships might have been missed in Ch 9 of CC1. Make sure students can identify the part to whole relationships in percents. The diagrams will help students connect the part whole relationships to equivalent ratios and percents.
Continue questioning students about part whole relationships, what quantities represent in the situation, and how each connects to a percent relationship.

Assigning 5-23 to teams or inviduals prior to the lesson will help students recall information from CC1.  Problem 5-25 is a great team problem to use for collaboration. Be sure to watch for students written answers and encourage when only giving a fraction to relook at what the quesiton is asking them. In the learning log, students are enouraged to reflect on questions they still have about probability.
Have students work on 5-34 (a-c) prior to in person learning. Make sure you take time to go through 5-35 with students as this can be very challanging for students.

Draw attention to the terminology of dependent and independent in problem 5-44 (c) when working with problem 5-45 in class. Students should walk away from this lesson with a clear understanding of the differences between dependent and indepent in relationship to probabliity.
Students make predictions and organize their thoughts in a table, then test those predictions.

Students will be introduced to a tree diagram in order to organize outcomes when there are  more than one taking place at the same time.  students should be able to identify the three stragegies that could be used with probability. Systematic List, Probability Table, and Probability Trees. This can be used in a learning log or even a digital presenation shared with a team.

If time allows, doing problem 5-80 would be a great way to check understanding of outcomes of events where they are not equally likely.  If time is short, be sure to get to 5-81 where there is a stop light problem and allow for students to have discourse on this problem.

This section introduces the 5-D process, which is a very important process for students to learn with probelm solving and is used later on in the course. However, keep in mind that the 5-D process is formally introduced in Lesson 5.3.2. For this lesson really focus on your students taking a word problem and interpreting relationships between quantiies in words as well as drawings or diagrams, which is the first D of the 5-D process.

Have teams complete 5-104 prior to coming together for the lesson. Then use their work to help go through problem 5-105 and introduce the reamining steps of the 5-D process in problem 5-106. Be sure to have students understand what all 5 D's stand for.

Use the target questions as a way for teams to focus on what their learning goal is for this lesson. These work great in a closure activity at the end of the lesson. While you might only do one together, if time permits have them work independently, with a partner, or collaboratively on a google doc for the remainder problems in this lesson.

This lesson give students more time for practice with the 5-D process but be mindful on how variables are used. Use 5-129 and 5-130 to help set students up with success in their learning. If they are done large group setting, ask purposeful questions to get students to identify how to apply variables into the problem.

cpm_modules.reduced_year_problems: 5-143 share out only what teams have created and their thought process
cpm_modules.notes: With this activity, make sure that each team has a different 5-D probelm they are working on. If you have more teams then problems, assign two teams to one problem but don't allow them to work together. They may answer the probelm in the 5-D process different and it allows for great  large group conversation.
6-1 and 6-3 really gets students thinking about why expression are equal.  It is important to use correct terminology when starting this lesson out.

6-13 will need students to draw back to part d of 6-12, which is ok. Pay close attention to problem 6-15 and be sure to read the teacher notes in order to draw attention to the stoplight problem. It is important that students understand what is being shown in this problem.

Boundary point is introduced here, and students may not understand at first. Be sure to use  visuals to help out. If you don't have algebra tiles for students, have them use the etools. You might need to spend time showing students how to us the etools for this in a digial setting.
Be sure that students are setting up the number lines and drawing the inequalities correctly.
This lesson starts to get students to shift from the inequality equation mat with a ? into the Equation Mat with an = sign.  Be sure students see the difference between them.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Solving Equations and the Distributive Property
cpm_modules.notes: Checking the work is a key factor here and be sure to really pay close attention to how students are recording their work. This does play into may other lessons in this course and future courses.
If time allows,have students complete 6-71 prior to the lesson. It will help build some background information and prior thinking on this concept to the lesson.

This lesson has a focus where it draws back in from chapter 5 the 5D process and starts to apply that knowlege to word problems where students can start to write equations built from what they already know and use variables for the unknown.

Potentially you could build google slides, jamboard, google doc, ect. Assign each slide/page/board per team to show their work in solving their part of 6-103. Then either do a gallary walk, have their team record their reasoning, or create a digital display for explaining how they did their work.

In problem 6-116, parts c and e really start to look at what happens when expressions are simplified and applied a zero to the concept. If time allows, the rest of 6-116 is good material to do. Potentially it helps deepen the understanding of solutions. Could be done in a team asychrounously.

This lesson wraps everything up in this Chapter. You could potentially do a participation quiz and have it all done in groups. It could be done by using a digial platform where students can gallary walk when done to other teams work to leave comments.

This has a very hands on lesson. Two options to help get the lesson content to your students.  Option 1 = video yourself and some colleagues doing this and give to students to use. Option 2 = use the charts in the teachers notes but be mindful of the questions asked and how much you want to give them without explanation as to how the charts were created.

Important that you read the Teacher Notes and have discussions around answers students came up wth for 7-16 and 7-17. Be sure to assign Review and Preview problems 7-22 and 7-26 as they will be used in this chapter and chapter 8

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Equations with Fraction and Decimal Coefficients
cpm_modules.notes: Consider swapping 7-44 for another review and preview problem. Or potentially as a weekly bonus problem
7-51 could be done prior to the lesson with some individual think time.
If time, 7-87 is a good problem to do as well.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Finding Missing Information in Proportional Relationships

cpm_modules.notes: With this lesson, it has students using tools to measure and class data. One way to shorten this or use this in a compelte virtual setting, is the teacher could front load problem 8-2 for the students with a video on doing the measuring for students to watch. Then create a set of data for probem 8-4 if you wish to add class data into student tables. This could work just fine if you skpped 8-4 and had them just use their data for 8-5 if needed to.
Consider having students do 8-19 with the previous day as a homework check. That could help lead a discussion on 8-20 to start the lesson out.

8-44 was added into this as it is the students' Learning Log for this section. This would be a good way to formativly assess student understanding and see if any additional problems need to be added back in.

cpm_modules.reduced_year_problems: 8-66 through 8-71 but only have a few, like 2-3 examples for the longer problems.
cpm_modules.notes: This lesson is filled with vocabulary. You can do one of two things. You can do large group facilitated of these problems, or have teams create definitions for these words and share out with the teams in a Gallary Walk

For this lesson, there is a large activity for problem 9-2, and it is importatnt that you can find a way to do this problem. For some it might be simply a video of it that students watch prior to class or teacher modeled in class with thought provoking questions.
There are two videos you could show as things to reflect on and watch in teams or individually.
This is a good lesson for the Desmos Activity. It could be done with teams, teacher facilitated, or individual.
This is a good lesson for the Desmos Activity. It could be done with teams, teacher facilitated, or individual.

These lessons work as a review of some of the major theads and topics in this course.  If you are limited to time you meet with your students, consider putting this into a digital gallary walk on google slides where teams work together on problems or even put them into a Desmos Activity and build in pacing.  Then have teams view each other's presenatation on the combined document, such as google slides. Split your teams up so that you have representation on both of these. This could also be done as an assessment for end of the year with students.

The Algebra Walk is one of the most memorable problems for students. It is not really possible to do unless you are together in class

If you do have time in class together, this is a "must do" lesson.  You will find that graphing takes on a much more concrete understanding for students who have graphed themselves.

This lesson is meant to be done in class together.  If it you must do this lesson remotely, have a set of data (for problem 1-25) prepared before your time with students.

If you choose to do this lesson as written, it needs to be done synchronously (in person or video) as it introduces Algebra Tiles.  If your choice is to do this lesson asynchronously, it is best to have a .pdf of the different tile names for students.

Problem 2-15 is an extension that can be used as a challenge problem or an asynchronous individual problem.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Line of Best Fit Equation & Association
cpm_modules.notes:
Consider the acronym FiDOS to help students recall all aspects of association.  
F = form: linear or non-linaer, 
i = place holder, 
D = direction: increasing (+), decreasing (-), stays the same (0), 
O = outliers: yes or no, and if yes, where, and why might that be?
S = strength: strong, moderate, weak

Consider skipping or combining parts of 8.1.1 through 8.1.3 to compare and contrast Simple Interest (Linear) and Compound Interest (Exponential Growth) so students have seen it before they study exponential growth more in depth in Core Connections Algebra Chapters 5 and 7.

If you do not think that will work well with your students, you could give data from Suggested Lesson Activity to save time. Check out the SHARING tab in your eBook for this lesson. cpm_modules.core_concepts: Connecting solving equations and multiple representations
cpm_modules.notes: Closure: 1 or 2 Days: VT: assign teams 4 or 5 problems. Teams prepare presentations on one problem to share with class in-person or virtually (through videos or shared whiteboard or slideshow).

Normally a 2-day lesson but could be pushed to one if needed with 7-1 serving as a whole-group launch before breakout rooms. Having the Resource Manager share their screen with Desmos will foster better team discussion as teams investigate the effect of values of b on an exponential graph. Graphing calculators can also be used, but you then run up against the challenge of facilitating and troubelshooting key sequences of many!? HW problem 7-11 reviews many of the ideas from Ch. 6, so if time, you may want to assign it in class and discuss. Check out the SHARING tab in your eBook for this lesson.

7-79 can be very time-consuming for teams with less then productive results. Instead, it can be used as a quick self-assessment for students in the opening few minutes, followed by a whole-group conversation about the process involved in writing the linear equation. That will lead into a class walk-through of extending that process to exponential equations in 7-80. Ensuring the class has a decent game plan before breakout rooms will enable your teams of students to function more efficiently and persevere through a bit of productive struggle (vs unproductive struggle) when writing exponential equations. One student sharing their screen will foster more team discussion as well. Circulation is then key for 7-82 and 7-83. Feel free to call a Huddle back to the main room to briefly share some information in regards to fractional exponents.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Writing the equation of a line
cpm_modules.notes:
Day 1: Slope-Point: VT: 2-75 to 79; IS: 2-81 (LL).
Day 2: Point-Point: IS: Desmos activities listed in ebook Extra Activity lesson.

8-1 can be used as a self-check for students for product=sum. A brief discussion as a whole group will get the lesson launched quickly. Do 8-2 (a) together as a class before releasing into teams. Come back as a whole group to debrief on 8-4 to ensure students identify Casey's pattern (which is needed for tomorrow's lesson). Closure can be done verbally in a live session or on a shared Google doc, with each team sharing what they figured out about the two diagonals.

The 8-55 Student eTool and 8-55 Resource Page will be very helpful resources for the teamwork problem when complete remotely.After teams complete the work on the balloon toss, coming back together as a whole class to discuss the "winners" will create some teachable moments. Those can lead right into 8-56 as a whole class discussion. Students may struggle with the initial instructions of "Write an explicit equation..." so be prepared to lead a discussion on how they see the pattern, what parts of each pattern are consistent, what parts change, etc. As a class, you can get at the explicit rule be piecing together the parts. If there's no time for 8-56, it can be skipped.  Multiple Representations Resource Page

Teams can jump right into the first 2 teamwork problems will little to no guidance, for it's reviewing a big chapter 8 concept. Definitely call teams back together in the main room (if teaching synchronously) for a demo of completing the square with tiles in 9-3, and then transition to using a generic rectangle instead. Doing 9-4a together as a whole class is advised, leaving teams to complete 9-4b together. If students are doing this asynchronously, a videotaped demo of physically completing a square of tiles would go a long way for them to start to really conceptualize this. Fun way to teach completing the square!

Although 9-44 can be fun if carried out as designed, the virtual setting may make this difficult. Another option is to have each student to write a solution for each inequality in parts a-h and then have a class discussion for each one if more than one solution is actually possible. That class discussion will lead into 9-45 nicely and provide some guidance for when teams start working on 9-46. Check out the SHARING tab in your eBook for this lesson.

Making a resource page with coordinate grids ahead of time for 9-67 and 9-68 will save a lot of instructional time so students can simply focus on graphing the boundary line on the provided grid and sharing the appropriate region.

The graph from homework problem 9-84 is needed for the first problem today, so having a student's work from that problem ready and available for a whole-class discussion would ensure all students are able to do 9-89 virtually. Students could use Desmos for 9-90 and draw a sketch of the graph then, shading the overlap region. Coming back together for 9-91 might be helpful if you plan to go about the testing portion of regions differently. Yes, you can test a point in each of the 5 regions. OR students can still test a point on one side of the line and lightly shade. Then they can test a another point either inside or outside the parabola and lightly shade. Finally, they can make note of the overlap with a darker shading.) The use of the provided resource page is crucial in keeping teamwork moving efficiently for 9-92. Markers may help students see the final overlapped region.

This lesson is somewhat of an island here in Ch. 10. If you are pressed for time, this lesson could be skipped and revisited at the end of the school year (if time permits). Could be combined with some mini-lessons on probability and computations with probability as well. Otherwise, if you take it on, a class walk-through 10-1 to 10-4 would suffice.

A Swap Meet for 10-23 is a good opportunity for teams to see other strategies for rewriting equations. Walk through 10-24 as a class or else it'll take up way too much time. After talking through this problem together, teams should be fairly independent with 10-25. 10-26 can be optional. If you do not complete 10-26 in class, do not assign 10-27 for homework.

Typically a 2-day lesson. See teacher notes in your eBook for how to break it up across 2 days. Notes here are for carrying out the lesson in 1 day. If you are short on time, you could skip 10-100. Note that 10-102 is a LL and could be done at the end of the lesson instead of in the middle (or assigned as independent work). Create a ready-made resource page for any required graphing to save time (or simply have teams verify their solutions by entering the system into Desmos).

2-day lesson that can be explored on the surface level in 1 day. This lesson is part of the CCSS but is also an overview of a topic students will cover in more depth in CCA2. Do not expect mastery of this concept at this time. Can be abbreviated if needed due to time/pacing constraints placed on this school year. Providing each team with a ready-made resource page of 5 coordinate grids will enable teams to focus more on the transformations themselves and not waste a ton of class time setting up coordinate grids.

There are extensive Lesson Notes for this lesson. Read them carefully and be well prepared. If you give students a table to collect their data in an organized fashion, it will save a ton of time. If virtual and collecting data is near impossible, providing the data to each team works, too. They can still complete the last 4 bullet points of 11-26 with the data provided. Providing data will keep the lesson to 1 day vs 2. The Learning Log is an integral part of the lesson, so leave plenty of time for students to start it (you may want to build it into your closure). If you do not finish it during class, begin the next lesson with it.

Do not omit this section, but you can pick and chose which lessons to include.  Provides closure for Chapters 1 through 11 and offers practice with most of the major concepts studied in this course.  The activities in this section require students to connect different strands of knowledge in order to solve complex problems.  These activities are independent of each other and may be used in any order.  In fact, if time is limited, most of the activities in Section 12.2 can be used at any point after Chapter 10 in order to help students review for an end-of-the-course final.  It is important to do the problems yourself to see what is involved in each activity.  Indicate which of the problems from Section 12.2 students use as your portfolio entry.  This could be used a course assessment.

Section 6.2 provides closure for Chapters 1 through 6 and offers practice with the major concepts studied so far.  Section 6.2 is intended to wrap up the first semester of instruction.  Activities in this section require students to weave together different threads of knowledge in order to solve complex problems.  The activities in this section are independent of each other and may be used in any order.  During the activities, students consolidate what they know, apply concepts in new ways, and identify questions for further exploration.  The problems require students to work with their teams to identify the tools and strategies they will use to reach a solution.  There is no need to complete all the days of this section. You may have to make the decision that you cannot conduct all of them based on time constraints. You can select the ones that best meet the needs of your class in wrapping up the semester.  The lessons in this section can be done in any order.  You can combine them as you choose.  It is important to do the problems yourself to see what is involved in each activity.  Indicate which of the problems from Section 6.2 students use as your portfolio entry.  This could be used a mid-course assessment.

Since Chapter 7 often marks the beginning of a new semester or course, Section 7.1 acts as a launch to Chapters 7 through 12. In this section, students explore four challenging activities that introduce some of the focus areas that lie ahead and motivate new teams to establish good communication and learning habits. They are designed to pique the interest of students so that they look forward to future lessons. If you have time for only one of these lessons, we recommend that you have students complete Lesson 7.1.3.

This is the section where they solidify proof.  Spend time here with discussions, helping them to critique each other's prrofs in a positive way.  A good way of doing this is to spend time each day having a student or two present their proofs to the class.  You can pick two that do it a different way (even if it is only notation or the order of statements that differ) and have them discuss it.  Maybe in a Jamboard or other shared space (use Shared Rough Draft and Virtual Gallery Walk and Jamboard).  You can pull ideas from the "My Favorite No" video, having students always identify something the proof did well, even if it wasn't completely successful.
Chapter Pocket Question Cards (Also under Teacher Tab under Teacher Resources) are very helpful.

cpm_modules.notes: Note that Section 8.1: Angles and Area of a Polygon does not directly meet a CCSS standard. However, this section is not optional and cannot be skipped because it builds a foundation for the remainder of the course. For example, later, the formula for the area of a circle is directly a result of the work with the area of regular polygons in Section 8.1. A similar process is used for the formula for the volume of a cone.

Students examine the relationships between areas of similar figures.  Students discover that the ratio of the areas between similar figures is equal to the square of the zoom factor (or linear scale factor). Chapter 3 was r, these two days are connecting it to r^2, later there will be a lesson on r^3, it's tempting to skip this for now, but don't.  It's good to have this here, so it sits with them awhile before 9.1.5 when r^3 comes up.
Students extend this generalization to finding the area and perimeter of a regular polygon with an infinite number of sides in order to develop the area and circumference formulas for a circle.

Students study solids, including polyhedra and cylinders.  This investigation begins by looking at different ways to represent three-dimensional solids with cubes.  Pocket Question Cards (Editable Google Doc Pocket Questions are in the Printable Resources Tab), these would be a great resource to help with prompts for virtual chats.  You may wish to omit some of the lessons in this chapter depending on the specific needs of your students.  Before skipping any lesson, please refer to the Course Timeline in the “Course Preparation” tab in the  Teacher Tab of this eBook.  In place of real blocks, there are virtual blocks in the eTools https://technology.cpm.org/general/3dblocks/

Students transition back to working with two-dimensional shapes as they investigate constructions.  While work with a compass and a straightedge may not seem immediately connected to the curriculum to this point, this section provides a context for a wealth of review opportunities, including reflections, quadrilateral theorems, congruence, similarity, parallel line theorems, etc.  Teachers are strongly discouraged from skipping this section, particularly if standardized testing or final exams are approaching.  However, an excellent virtual tool to use is Desmos Geometry Tool (https://learn.desmos.com/geometry).  https://www.desmos.com/geometry  Creating and justifying the constructions requires students to connect concepts from many of the earlier sections.  Additionally, this section helps students to build a more concrete understanding of the role a radius plays in a circle, which will help them in the study of circles, arcs, and chords in Chapter 10 and 11.

The relationships between angles, arcs, and line segments in a circle will be investigated to develop “circle tools” that can help solve problems involving circles.  You may wish to omit some of the lessons in this chapter depending on the specific needs of your students.  Before skipping any lesson, please refer to the Course Timeline in the “Course Preparation” tab in the  Teacher Tab of this eBook.

You might be able to skip this section depending upon your student's needs or unless accountability tests mandate its inclusion. Area models and two-way tables provide the basis for calculating conditional probabilities and determining whether events are independent.

Some sample spaces are so large that models cannot easily represent them.  Based on the Fundamental Principle of Counting, other formulas for permutations and combinations are developed that can be used to solve more complex problems.  You might be able to skip this section depending upon your student's needs or unless accountability tests mandate its inclusion. This will help you plan how much to cover from this section.  Section 10.3: Permutations and Combinations (6 days). Using permutations and combinations addresses a CCSS advanced “plus” standard (S.CP.9+), intended for students that will later take advanced courses such as calculus, advanced statistics, or discrete mathematics. Note that Lesson 10.3.1 in the following course, CPM Core Connections Algebra 2, uses very simple combinations and permutations to develop the binomial theorem. However, homework problems prior to this lesson in Core Connections Algebra 2 review the use of permutation and combinations, allowing teachers to quickly teach the topic if it was omitted in the prior Geometry course.

If you are short on time, this chapter could be skipped and you can go directly to 12.2 to begin course closure.  Before you omit this section consider if accountability tests mandate its inclusion. You may wish to omit some of the lessons in this chapter depending on the specific needs of your students.  Before skipping any lesson, please refer to the Course Timeline in the “Course Preparation” tab in the  Teacher Tab of this eBook. Lesson 11.1: Platonic Solids (3 days). This lesson connects 2-dimensional reasoning (interior angles that meet at a vertex) with 3-dimensional objects. Students build the Platonic Solids, understand why these are the only solids with faces that are congruent, regular polygons, and describe polyhedra using the number of faces. This enrichment is not required by the CCSS standards. Section 11.1 starts with an investigation of polyhedra, by having students generate all the possible Platonic solids (tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, and icosahedron).  This activity introduces students to interesting new structures, which helps launch the investigations of pyramids, cones, and spheres in Lessons 11.1.3, 11.1.4, and 11.1.5.

If you are short on time, this chapter could be skipped and you can go directly to 12.2 to begin course closure.  Before you omit this section consider if accountability tests mandate its inclusion. Section 11.2 uses a geological context to drive an investigation of circles, tangents, and secants.

If you are short on time, this section could be skipped and you can go directly to 12.2 to begin course closure. Before you omit this section consider if accountability tests mandate its inclusion. You may wish to omit some of the lessons in this chapter depending on the specific needs of your students.  Before skipping any lesson, please refer to the Course Timeline in the “Course Preparation” tab in the  Teacher Tab of this eBook.  In Section 12.1 students look at the connections between algebra and geometry through the study of conic sections.  The first two lessons look at circles and their equations. The second two lessons are an introduction to other conic sections.  Lesson 12.1.3 meets “Plus Standards”.

eBook Scavenger Hunt Desmos | Ideally, this lesson will be done as a synchronous meeting, as it is the first lesson of the year, and it is important to get to know each other and to set the norms and expectations for the year. In a virtual meeting, you can assign each team to a different screen on this shared Jamboard for 1.1.1. Responses to 1-4 can be collected through the chat function of a virtual meeting room or with student homeworks. Be sure to provide your students with a way to create a Parent Graph Organizer. They can print copies of the Resource Page as needed, or import it to a note-taking app such as Notability. They will need to keep this somewhere for reference throughout the year. 
Refer to the Supplemental Resources eBook Teacher–Virtual Collaboration–Rough Draft Thinking tab for more information on collaboration using shared Google files. Join the Teaching CPM Remotely–Planning for Getting Started Remotely–Chapter 1 module at professionallearning.cpm.org for more support as you get started this year.

The type of video you use will determine what problems teams are assigned and how they go about working on those problems. cpm_modules.core_concepts: Introduction to Graphical Displays of Data
cpm_modules.notes:
eBook Scavenger Hunt Desmos
MN 1.1.1 - Vocabulary | Ideally, this lesson could be done as a synchronous meeting, as it is the first lesson of the year and important to spend time getting to know your students and setting norms and expectations. (Check out the Planning for Getting Started Remotely - Chapter 1 and the Teaching CPM Remotely modules at professionallearning.cpm.org for many ideas.) In a virtual meeting, get to know your new class by collecting some interesting data together using polls or a Google Form. Students can create representations of the results and hold up their work to their screen to share with the class, or work in breakout rooms on a shared Google Jamboard to then share with the class. The Graphical Displays and Data Desmos activity by TK is an alternative which could be used in an asynchronous setting, or as additional review by students independently. This activity can also be found in the Sharing Tab on the ebook.
MN 1.1.2 - Displays of Data | Creating Histograms by Desmos will walk students through building histograms and can be used in place of 1-10.

MN 1.1.3 - Shape of Distributions
Desmos Box Plots | Use the RI Stats 1.1.3 for this lesson  (be sure to edit it to fit your own class's needs and upload your own teacher video introduction). This activity can be modified to fit other lessons as needed when you are teaching remotely. You can use the activity during a synchronous meeting, which will allow you to pause the class and present from your screen as appropriate for discussions. It can also be use asynchronously, although we encourage you to remind students to work with their classmates so they can discuss the mathematics and support one another's learning.

MN 1.2.1 - Boxplots & Measures of Center
Using the TI-84 for stats videos | Desmos Activity - Choosing Mean and Median by TK (follows the CPM book problems) This activity can be also be found in the Sharing Tab on the ebook.

| You can save time in class by presetting the data for your students and saving the CPM Stats tool state. Students can use this 1-37 eTool to answer many of the questions in this lesson. Students will not be asked to calculate MAD or standard deviation on the exam for more than a couple of points, but it will help students understand the meaning of these measures of variability if they calcultate them once by hand. Use a shared Google space for teams to collaborate, or create a Desmos template to collect selected answers if students must work asynchronously.
MN 1.2.3 - Formulas for Variance | Desmos Activity - Sample vs. Population Variance by TK (follows CPM book problems)

Desmos Polygraph for histogam shapes | Have students do the activity in 1-53 at home and record their data in a shared Google Sheet (would be fun to collect the data during a synchronous meeting!). You could also create a simple Google Form to collect data and then share the results in a tabular form with your students (assign this as homework several days in advance so you will have enough data when you need it). Instruct teams to complete the problem on a shared Jamboard which can then be shared with the class for a Virtual Gallery Walk.
MN 1.3.1 | Desmos Activity - Percentiles by TK (follows the CPM book problems) This activity can be found in the Sharing Tab on the ebook.
MN 1.3.2 | Desmos Activity - Z-scores by TK (follows the CPM book with a little modification) This activity can be found in the Sharing Tab on the ebook.

MN 1.3.3 | Use the Collecting Class Data Desmos Activity to collect student estimates. In this activity, you can replace the list R with R-1 or any transformation desired. You can copy the dotplot line to have two dotplots, one for R (the original responses) and one for the transformed list. If you are working asynchronously, use a Google Form or shared class spreadsheet to collect responses from students. The responses can be copied from the Google Form and pasted into a Desmos calculator to share with your class so they can answer the questions using that data.

MN 2.1.1 | Desmos Polygraph: Scatterplots activity requires more than one student to be online at the same time. If done in a synchronous setting, have teams play after problem 2-4 for 2-3 rounds then again after problem 2-5.

MN 2.1.2 | *Give students the data collected from the teacher's notes. Provide a Shared Rough Draft space so teams can collaborate virtually, whether they are working synchronously or asynchronously. The Supplemental Resources eBook has samples for this in the Teacher – Virtual Collaboration – Rough Draft Thinking tab.

MN 2.1.3 | Use the Desmos activity linked in the Teacher Notes for this lesson. It is written with pacing guides, and intended to be used with a teacher facilitating, so you may have to modify this if you are working asynchronously.

MN 2.1.4 | The resource page for 2-28 is a graph. You can share this Desmos Graph 2-28 with your students or they can print the Resource Page using the link in their eBooks. Collect team's responses to 2-28 (a) and graph them on the same graph (start with the shared graph of the data) so students can compare and discuss 2-29. The Teacher Notes for the lesson has a link to two versions of a Desmos activity which you might use to replace 2-31 or this entire lesson, depending on your class. 2-32 is a good review, consider using it for a formative assessment.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Using Technology to Find the LSRL
cpm_modules.notes: MN 2.1.5 | You will need to provide students with instructions for 2-36 (Ti). Problem 2-37 shows students how to caclulate the LSRL with Desmos- AP does not allow Desmos. Adjust as nessesary. Do not spend too much time with this, as the AP will not ask students to create an LSRL, only to be able to interpret the computer output for one.

MN 2.2.1 | This math notes box is something that AP students need to know. You will reference it in the future. The e-tool for 2-46 is NOT directly linked in the e-book, you will need to provide the link to your students.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Behavior of Correlation and the LSRL
cpm_modules.notes: | Check the "Share" tab as well.
| Use the RI Stats 2.2.3 Desmos activity for problems 2-63 to 67. 2-68 would be a good problem to have your students write out and turn in to you.
MN 2.2.4 | You may want to look up other correlations and check the "Share" tab for this lesson. Students may enjoy coming up with spurious correlations of their own.
MN 2.2.5 | Could use the other problems as a closure actvity on another day.

MN 3.1.1
Use CCG Checkpoint 9A in the Supplemental eBook if a review of probability models is needed. | Open the lesson with a shared Google Jamboard prepared with a Venn diagram using 2 mutually exclusive events (eg: A:I am an only child and B:I have more than one sibling). Ask students to write their initial on the diagram in the appropriate space. Use this as a launch to remind students that P(A and B)=0 for mutually exclusive events. A shared Google file is a nice place for students working synchronously to work through these problems together. Teams can copy diagrams from the ebook, paste them in as a picture on the shared whiteboard, and draw on them to fill them in as they discuss the models together.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Association and Conditional Relative Frequency Tables
cpm_modules.notes: MN 3.1.2 | This lesson can work in Desmos if it is taught virutally. RI Stats 3.1.2 can be edited to fit your class's needs, and includes tables students can fill in for frequency tables. (You may also want to copy screens from this activity to use in your own Desmos activities for other lessons).

MN 3.1.3 | While students may have seen set notation in the past, it may have been a while. Consider an exit ticket to check for understanding. There are several virtual assessments such as Kahoot or Quizzez that have several quick activities that could be used after this lesson if you have time.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Relative Frequency Tables and Conditional Probabilities
cpm_modules.notes: MN 3.1.4 | 3-31 can be a good exit ticket or problem to be used later for a review if nessessary. While this lesson appears short, the ideas can be challenging, so checking for understanding will be important.
MN 3.1.5 | Start the class with this Shifty Shauna Desmos activity which is embedded in the RI Stats 3.1.5 activity. You can edit the 3.1.5 activity to include your personal notes.

| Tip: copy and paste a diagram from the ebook to a virtual whiteboard so your teams can write on the diagram without having to redraw it. (Please do not share copyrighted material such as eBook images outside of your classroom!)

| This is a good lesson for a Shared Rough Draft team document. After teams have had time to look at the problems and try them (not solve all of them), assign one problem for each team to complete and prepare a video or virtual poster to explain it to the class. Assign a time limit for presentations so you will have enough time for all of them to be presented.

| Idea for class simulations in remote environment: create a shared class spreadsheet, assign each student a row number, and have them enter the results of their coin flips in there. Use the sum() operator to obtain the class totals. If the class is asynchronous, assign the simulation the night before so you have the totals ready when needed. If you get too many results near the expected value, consider challenging the class and finding out if they really did the flipping! You can also create a simple Google Form asking students the number of successes they got for their trials, and use the spreadsheet created with the resonses to sum the total for the class.

| In this lesson, teams are going to pick out their own research questions to work through. This could be done individually but it would be helpful for students to have the opportunity to talk about their research questions with their teams. Problems 4-4, 4-5, and 4-6 are not core problems but could be used later as a formative assessment. It could be tempting to skip having students work through their own research questions, but if you can give them the time, it pulls together concepts and give students ownership of their learning.

cpm_modules.core_problems: TF: 4-13 through 4-15; Team: 4-16 and 4-17; IS: 4-21 to 4-24
cpm_modules.notes: MN 4.1.2 | Use the 4.1.2 Data Collection Template to collect data for your class. 4-20 asks students to choose their sampling methods. If you are having your students work through their own project, you'll need to include this problem.

MN 4.1.3 | Nearly all of the problems in this lesson require class data collection. Consider using something like the template created in 4.1.2 and using Desmos to display the data, or use the generic e-tool at stats.cpm.org. 4-30 continues to lead students through their own survey project started in lesson 4.1.1.

MN 4.1.4 | If you have a way to allow students to post in discussions asychonously, problems 4-38 and 4-39 could be done this way alone to save time. 4-37 could possibly be done this way as well, but would be better as a teacher-led problem.
| This lesson can be safely skipped if you need more time elsewhere. 4-47 asks students to reflect on their survey project if you are having them do that on the side.
MN 4.2.1 | *needs whole group conclusion - teams are coming up with ways to make the experiment better and those considerations need to be taken into account for the next problem.
MN 4.2.2 | This lesson is greatly enhanced by the etool linked in the lesson. This lesson could be done asychronously if needed but team discussion will help tremendouly with these ideas.
MN 4.2.3 | There is a great etool on the stats.cpm.org page here to do this lesson virtually.
| This experiment needs to be done live. Consider coming back to it after the AP test, especially if you are with your students at that time.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Relative Frequency Histograms and Random Variables
cpm_modules.notes: MN 5.1.1 | To save time, the data for 5-1 and 5-3 are pre-entered into the Univariate Data Explorer at stats.cpm.org

MN 5.1.2 | 5-10 could be done in teams alone, but as it's the begining of a new concept, having some teacher interaction may help. If you are working remotely, you can use a Virtual Whiparound as closure.

MN 5.1.3 | Watch for the words "your teacher will..." in this lesson. You will need to provide some support for those problems. You can share these activities with your class to save time graphing: Desmos 5-17 and Desmos 5-19.  You can toggle the normal function off to start by clicking on the round blue symbol next to the function in the graph.

MN 1.3.1 (Percentiles) | This lesson includes brief instructions on how to use the Inverse Norm function on a Ti calculator in the introduction. Students will sometimes miss them; you may need to point them out.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: The Standard Normal Distribution and z-Scores
cpm_modules.notes: MN 1.3.2 (z-Scores) | As we get further and further into the course, there is less and less we can cut as the College Board is not altering the AP test. Be careful to still allow time for student understanding.

| This lesson could be assigned indivdually or used like a more traditional CPM closure from other CPM courses. Virtually, you could also have teams complete assigned problems in a Google JamBoard and to a virtual Gallery Walk for closure. There are a lot of other ideas in the Supplemental Resource e-Book, so please check that out too!

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Mean and Variance of Descrete Random Variables
cpm_modules.notes: MN 6.1.1, expected value is in CCG and Int 2 | Although the concepts of descrete and continuous along with expected value have been covered in previous courses, this lesson takes them deeper into those topics. Students could also possibly do this lesson individually if needed.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Linear Combinations of Independent Random Variables
cpm_modules.notes: | The idea that X+X and 2X are different can cause students a bit of trouble here. 6-13 helps clear this up, but ciruclation during 6-12 to help address misconceptions is important.

MN 6.1.3 | Despite this lesson only consisting of two problems, it is a challenging topic. Be sure to check in on your teams while they are tackling 6-22. 6-23 is challenging; consider using it for your teams that need it.

| This lesson has a great set up! Be sure to do the "pretest" at the begining of the lesson as its sets the whole thing up. A Google Form may be a good way to present it to your class. Be sure they can reference it later to make sense of where the probabilities are coming from. If students are not familiar with Pascal's Triangle, the lesson and patterns will still go through, so do not worry about spending time reviewing the Triangle.

| Consider making 6-42 into some sort of class resouce (like a poster) as it will be refered to often. This lesson moves students from the combinations formula to the actual binomial function. If your students are not familiar with combinations, it is okay! It is not worth the time to fill in that gap as it's too limiting to be of much use (see problem 6-45).

MN 6.2.3 | The MN box in this lesson would make a good poster as descibed in 6-42 above. This lesson helps clarify the difference between pdf and cdf; the team problems could be done individually if you had to.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Shape, Center, and Spread of the Binomail Distrubution
cpm_modules.notes: MN 6.2.4 | Problem 6-66 is challenging. It may help to work out the problem in advance so that you can refer to it as you talk to your class.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Normal Aproximation to the Binomial Distribution
cpm_modules.notes: MN 6.2.5 | Consider using Google Sheets to collect team data for problem 6-74, then cut the table and instert it into Desmos to display for the class. Set up the graph with the two equations before hand. "Notice and Wonder" is what we're going for; encourage your students to look at the points and where they are in the graph.

MN 6.3.1, geometric series from earlier books | This lesson uses the idea of series to introduce the geometric distribution. If your students don't remember this concept, it's okay! Have them go back to the table and think about what is happening there. They should be able to move on once they see the pattern in the table.

MN 6.3.2 | While the stats book does not have traditional CPM chapter closures, this lesson is close. These problems could be done individually if nessesary as there is nothing new in this lesson. You could also apply any of the closure activities you may enjoy from other classes such as stations, scavenger hunts, etc.

MN 7.1.1 | This lesson requires physical manipulations - the lesson requires counting beans, beads, or candies. If your class is 100% virtual, ask your students to buy bags of candies and find the manufacturers percentages to compare. There is also a simulator here that could be used. The simulator is set to have each color come up 16.6% and when you run the program, you can see the "actual" colors of candies in the counter box. While it may not be as powerful as doing this in person as the lesson suggests, it does still help get the idea across.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Simulating Sampling Distributions of Sample Proportions
cpm_modules.notes: MN 7.1.2 | In this lesson, the idea of sample variation is further fleshed out. Problem 7-11 could be done using Red Light, Green Light -it's important to see that students are correct there. The suggested lesson in the Teacher Notes has some good ideas that could be done virtually.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Formulas for the Sampling Distributions of Sample Proportions
cpm_modules.notes: MN 7.1.3 | Even though 7-24 through 7-26 are not core problems, they are useful practice and help cement the concepts. You may want to assign them for homework or add it onto your teamwork load if possible. The Math Notes boxs are both very important; often on the AP test, problems are presented that don't quite fit the criteria. If students don't check the criteria, they will miss easy points and waste valuable time.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Confidence Interval for a Population Proportion
cpm_modules.notes: | This lesson relies on teams working together to come up with their team "trashketball" average. This team average is used several times throughout this section. There is an online free game called "hook a duck" that could be used to work this out. You could have your kids go into their team rooms and do the activity as a team - track how many ducks are hooked without a star before one is found, then average the games together - or the same thing could be done individually. If you refresh the page after a duck with a star is found, they are randomized again.

MN 7.2.2 | Students get more practice with confidence intervals in this lesson and there is a fantastic etool from CPM that helps show just what that means. While it's not a very flashy tool, it is a great visual that really explains what it means to have a  96% confidence interval.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Changing the Margin of Error in Confidence Intervals
cpm_modules.notes: | As your students reason through this lesson, circulate often to ensure they are on the right track. While they should be able to do these problems in their team, it's important not to simply abandon them. Consider a participation quiz for this lesson to help give your ciruculation more purpose and transparnecy with your class.

MN 7.2.4 | A large part of this lesson is the intrepetation of the calculations. As you are circultating, check to see that the interpreations are written out to the level the College Board expects - not writing to the College Board is a mistake that can lose your kids points on the AP exam. If your students are not sitting for the test, I would still expect them to write out as if they were; clear and consise explanations are a cornerstone of statistics.

MN 8.1.1 | In this lesson, students look deeper into a "sexist" situation and test their theories. There is a fantastic activity in the Sharing Tab that can replace the lesson although if you are able to do this one in person with the decks of cards, it can be very powerful and a lot of fun!

| In this lesson, the topic of trashketball is brought back. Students won't actually play trashket ball this time, but use the idea of averages and reality to work through an entire hypothesis test. The notation is challenging on a computer, but stick with it! Students need to be able to use and understand it.

MN 8.1.3 | This lesson continues on with hypothesis testing. The problems can be done by teams alone as long as you are able to cirucluate effectively - check for notation, understanding, and that conditions are met. Note that this lesson brings up the Presidential elelction of 2012 - think about how you might broach the topic of the current election or if you even want to. Type 1 and Type 2 errors are broached but not mentioned by name - that will come in the next lesson. The Math Notes are also pretty heavy for this lesson, so have an idea of how you'd like your students to organize/utilize the information.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Types of Error in Hypothesis Testing
cpm_modules.notes: MN 8.2.1 | The idea of Type I and Type II errors are explored more using the death penalty. It's important for students to have a chance to think about this on their own; a think-ink-pair may be a good way to allow for more voices to be heard. Inevitably, a student is going to look up the number of people executed and a conversation about real numbers and people can follow if you are okay with it. It may be something to think about before you do this lesson with your students, but please don't skip this lesson! It's a great way for students to remember the error types.

MN 8.2.2 | This lesson walks students through the Errors and Power etool - it's a fantastic interactive visual for students to work with. The Math Notes box is a good follow up/synthesis of the power of a test.

MN 8.3.1 | The more students have the opportunity to talk and collaborate together on this lesson, the better! There are some sticky problems here, and allowing students to talk through their ideas and what is going on will be helpful. Breakout rooms are great for that, if you are able to use them. Consider creating something like Google Slides for this lesson that allows other teams to go see what each are doing as a virtual "I Spy" can also help in this particular lesson.

MN 8.3.2 | If you ever have a virtual lesson go well becuase of something you have created or found, please don't hesitate to share it with other CPM Statistics teachers using the Sharing Tab to submit your ideas and resources!

MN 8.3.3 | This lesson is a great opportunity for you to share technology with your students. They are given a slew of problems to work through, but no new concepts are introduced. Showing students how to work with their Ti will help shave time off their AP test and keep an eye on Desmos; their statistical tools are getting better all the time. Students can't use Desmos on their AP tests yet, but it will come!

MN 9.1.1 | As you know, in AP courses, time is of the essence. That being said, it's important that your students know the material, not just been exposed to it. It may be time to start thinking about practice AP tests and additional practice that can help get your kids ready for the test. Think about your class and how time is being used. Is there 5 minutes in there somewhere that could be used for a review question? How are your students keeping their notes? Can those notes be used to review?

| Be sure to read the suggested lesson activity- circulation is vital for this lesson. Circulation in the virutal setting can be challenging - if you're able to do it at all. (Some schools do not allow break out rooms) Keep at it! It's a skill like any other.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: More Applications of Chi-Squared Goodness of Fit
cpm_modules.notes: MN 9.1.3 | Your students now have more than one inference procedure. Knowing what to use when is an important skill. Checking for conditions is a way that students can work out which procedure to use. Having more than a procedural knowlege will also help!

MN 9.2.1 | Trasketball is brought back in this lesson! While students won't be breaking out the paper and trashcans, if you can - a quick round or two of trashketball can be a fun way to start up. Students don't need their data, but will use data from our characters in the book. Problem 9-35 is not a core problem, but if you can fit it in as a formative assessment, it could be a good problem to see what your students can do with their new skills on their own.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Chi-Squared Test for Homogeneity of Proportions
cpm_modules.notes: MN 9.2.2 | As with any lesson that is heavy with team-only work, circulation and pocket questions will make the work go well. Let students think about and even wrestle with the concepts - please don't "steal" their learning as you listen in and help teams move forward.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Practicing and Recognizing Chi-Squared Inference Procedures
cpm_modules.notes: | Study Team and Teaching Strategies (STTS) are great ways to break up a lesson, add movement, check for understanding, and work with your students. It can be tempting to not worry about these things or even dismiss them becuase you are teaching upperclassmen. Please don't! They still need to get up and move around, talk with their peers, and have some fun! STTS can help with all of this in addition to trouble shooting and solving whatever issues may be coming up in your class. Many of these have been altered in the Supplemental ebook for virtual learning. If it's been a while since you've seen them, check them out! There are several new ones in addition to the virtual modifications.

cpm_modules.core_problems: TF: 10-1 through 10-7 + see note in column E.; Team: If your teams are strong, the core problems (10-1 through 10-7) may be done there. If not, they need to be teacher led.; IS: 10-9 through 10-12
cpm_modules.notes: | The Teacher's Notes have a few examples of what the distributions may look like. Check them out! If your students don't have access to the tech to make these distributions, you'll need to figure out a way to get these images to your students.

MN 10.1.2 | The closure portion of the lesson is always important, but in this lesson it's vital. Read the Teacher Notes for suggestions on what to summarize after your students have worked through this lesson.

MN 10.2.1 | This lesson needs students to be able to "take just a few" of something. To do this virtually, you may try to have your students do a quick poll, asking "How many is "just a few" jelly beans?" (or whatever small candy strikes your fancy) to see if you can create the class data needed to start this lesson.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Using the Normal Distribution with Means
cpm_modules.notes: MN 10.2.2 | This lesson is a grea review of a lot of previous work, but as it is very deep, it would still be better to have your teams work through these instead of asking individuals to do them. While 10-35 is not a core problem, I feel it is worth it - especially given that they may have limited time and access to the material.

MN 10.3.1 | If you are looking for a good definition of the degrees of freedom, the mathematical background of this lesson is very helpful. Also note that there is a short video embedded in the Confidence Interval of Means eTool that students will need to use for this lesson. It's directly linked in the ebook but you can also link to it if that's easier for you and your class. That same problem is a discovery experience for your students, so letting them do it in their teams will help them dig through what is going on, and ciruclation plus closure will be important.

MN 10.3.2 | Please read the Teacher Notes, it has a few great suggestions about what to look for in this lesson that are true no matter how your students are learning. Watch for the language your students are using - AP is very particular in what it's looking for to earn maximum credit.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: z-Tests and t-Tests for Populations Means
cpm_modules.notes: MN 10.3.3 | Watch for the prep on this lesson! You're going to need a class set of books. There are several of the classics that are available online; you may want to talk to your media staff or English department to see if they have any way to do this. Do go to the Teacher's Notes, there is a Google sheet available there to help with data collection.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Paired and Independent Data from Surveys and Experiments
cpm_modules.notes: | The virutal tool for this lesson can be found at stats.cpm.org - it's a lot of fun! But you are going to have to ask your students to record their data; the eTool does not allow you to see any of your students. You may want to share the tools seperately so that your discussions with your class are more authentic.

cpm_modules.core_problems: Team: 11-8 through 11-10 with circulation to check for correct writing; IS: 11-11 through 11-13
cpm_modules.notes: MN 11.1.2 | This lesson is purposfully short - use the rest of the time you have with your kids to review the three-step process to writing AP free response solutions and reviewing rubrics.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Tests for the Difference of Two Means
cpm_modules.core_problems: TF: 11-15 through 11-17; Team: 11-14 and 11-18; IS: 11-19 through 11-21
cpm_modules.notes: MN 11.1.3 | Make sure that you read the Teacher Notes for ideas on the formulas in this lesson - they are ones that students need to either memorize or at least know where to find them on the AP formula sheet. Watch out though! Not all of the formulas here need to be memorized, some are programmed into the calculator.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Two-Sample Mean Inference with Experiments and Two-Sample Confidence Intervals
cpm_modules.notes: MN 11.1.4 | Calculators are vital for this lesson - students using their Ti quickly and efficiently is a great place to pick up not only time on the AP test but also accuacy. The Technology Notes section for this lesson has some detailed instructions that are helpful. The Mathematical Background is also lengthy but a great read.

| This lesson is meant to be done by teams as you circulate and facilitate. These problems offer students an opportunity to look at inference in different situations, just like they will on the AP test. This may be a good time to give your students detailed feedback on their Free Response questions.  The Review/Preview also has a few multiple choice questions - these could be a good way to open your lesson tomorrow.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Identifying and Implementing an Appropriate Test
cpm_modules.notes: | Students will review concepts from chapters 7 through 11 in this final lesson of the chapter. It is another great review for the AP test - if students struggle here, it can be a good place for some in-the-moment remediation or a place where you can start thinking about offering. your students more guided study time outside of class.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Sampling Distribution of the Slope of the Regression Line
cpm_modules.notes: MN 12.1.1 | In this lesson, students are asked to recall experimental design and interpretaion of regression models. While this isn't new, it is a great place to double check your students' understanding of the vocabulary and to check their work for the details that matter. You might try running this lesson on a Google Jamboard if you havne't tried those in the past - they would let your students work but if you wanted, they can also send a "Spy" to other team's slides to see their work as well.

cpm_modules.core_concepts: Inference for the Slope of the Regression Line
cpm_modules.notes: MN 12.1.2 | You may want to lead your students through 12-9 as it's brining up formulas and ideas clear back from lesson 2.2.2 - the formula is given in the problem, but 2.2.2 was a long time ago! Make sure your students are verifying their assumptions and that they are writing to the level expected on the AP exam.

| Problem 12-18 sets up the question "What if linear isn't the best fit?" In problem 12-19, encourage your students to have a good reason why they want to use a different model instead of just guessing. There are some algebraic manipulations in this lesson that students may have "forgotten" how to do -watch out for any simple mistakes in their algebra.

MN 12.2.2 | It may have been a very long time since your student have used logarithms and may need some help with this lesson. If you can have a student show their work, it may be more powerful than you working through it. The Closure portion of the Teacher Notes has a great multiple choice AP-type problem that your students should see.
